_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 1151 verses across 62 terms

set.analysis_note: God's perception of the light registers an evaluative delight — his inner assessment finds the light genuinely good, pleasing to his discerning regard.
Again God surveys his work and his judgment finds it good — a repeated inner affirmation that what he has made satisfies his standard of excellence.
God's evaluative gaze upon the earth's vegetation produces the same verdict: it is good — his perception finds the fruitful order of creation pleasing.
God sees the luminaries ruling day and night and finds it good — his inner assessment approves the ordered governance of light and darkness he has established.
After creating sea creatures and birds, God perceives them as good — his evaluative delight affirms the teeming variety of living creatures as pleasing to him.
God's inner appraisal of the land animals he made finds them good — his judgment of pleasure and rightness extends to every creature on the ground.
God's own assessment identifies solitude as not good for the man — the absence of a companion is recognized as a deficiency, framing relational wholeness as essential to human flourishing.
The serpent frames knowing good and evil as a desired state — the inner faculty of moral discernment is presented as something to be gained, enticing the will toward forbidden knowledge.
The pleasing aroma of Noah's offering registers in God's inner being as soothing — it moves him to resolve in his heart to relent from judgment, showing the offering's power to evoke divine favor.
Abram defers to Sarai's will, telling her to do as she pleases with Hagar — the term here conveys what is good or fitting in Sarai's own judgment, granting her full personal agency.
The rhetorical question challenges any inner sense that something could be too extraordinary for God — wonder or impossibility is denied as a limit on the Lord's power to fulfill his word.
Lot offers his daughters for whatever the men please — the term signals unconstrained personal desire as the measure of action, revealing the moral extremity of Lot's attempt to protect his guests.
Abimelech invites Abraham to dwell wherever it seems good to him — the term points to Abraham's personal preference and satisfaction as the guide for choosing his place of residence.
Laban judges it better to give Rachel to Jacob than to another — his inner comparative assessment of what is more favorable guides his decision to accept Jacob's proposal.
Laban laments that Jacob fled without allowing him to send them off with mirth — the joy here is an outward celebration of farewell that the heart expects to express through festive music and song.
Shechem's delight in Dinah drove him to act swiftly — strong inner desire for her moved his will directly into urgent action, showing delight as a motivating force in the person.
The chief baker saw that the interpretation was favorable — his perception of Joseph's words as good lifted his spirit enough to prompt him to share his own dream.
God promises wonders he will do in Egypt — these are extraordinary acts that go beyond normal expectation, their astonishing character being what compels Pharaoh to release Israel.
Aaron's gladness at seeing Moses is located explicitly in his heart — joy at reunion arises from within, in the deepest inner seat of the person, as an instinctive response to a beloved brother.
The Israelites judge servitude in Egypt as better than dying in the wilderness — their fearful inner assessment ranks known bondage as more preferable than the peril now before them.
God is acclaimed as doing wonders — his incomparable acts inspire awe and astonishment among those who witness them, pointing to a divine character that exceeds all human categories.
Jethro rejoiced over all the good God had done for Israel — his inner gladness was stirred specifically by hearing of divine deliverance, making gratitude and wonder the source of his joy.
Jethro tells Moses that what he is doing is not good — his experienced judgment finds Moses's practice deficient, showing an evaluative inner discernment operating on behalf of Moses's wellbeing.
The burnt ram is described as a pleasing aroma to the Lord — the sacrificial act produces a soothing, favorable response in God, framing worship as something that genuinely satisfies divine regard.
Portions burned on the altar produce a pleasing aroma before the Lord — the offering is directed toward evoking divine favor, its soothing quality defining the relational effect of proper sacrifice.
The evening lamb offering is made as a pleasing aroma — the daily rhythm of sacrifice is oriented toward producing this soothing effect before God, sustaining an ongoing relationship of favor.

God promises to do marvels never seen in all the earth — these acts are so extraordinary that all who witness them will be filled with awe, their wonder confirming the uniqueness of God's covenant work.
The properly prepared burnt offering ascends as a pleasing aroma — the priestly act of complete consecration produces a soothing response in God, indicating his acceptance of the worshiper.
The bird offering, washed and fully burned, rises as a pleasing aroma to the Lord — the soothing quality of the sacrifice signals God's inner reception of it as acceptable and favorable.
The torn bird burned on the altar constitutes a pleasing aroma — even this smaller offering, correctly presented, produces a soothing acceptance before God when offered in obedience.
The memorial portion of the grain offering burned on the altar rises as a pleasing aroma — the priest's act transforms the worshiper's gift into something that soothes and satisfies God's regard.
The memorial portion of the grain offering burned on the altar is a pleasing aroma — the offering is received by God as soothing, showing that even grain presented rightly evokes his favor.

Firstfruits may be brought to the Lord but not offered on the altar as a pleasing aroma — the soothing effect is here withheld, setting a limit on which offerings produce divine acceptance through fire.
The peace offering portions burned on top of the burnt offering rise as a pleasing aroma — the soothing quality signals God's favorable reception of the combined act of communion sacrifice.
The fat burned on the altar rises as a pleasing aroma — the soothing effect on God is the stated purpose of burning the fat, marking it as the portion that belongs wholly to the Lord.
Fat from the sin offering burned on the altar produces a pleasing aroma to the Lord — the soothing quality of this act is paired with atonement, showing that it moves God toward forgiveness.
The memorial portion burned on the altar is a pleasing aroma — the priest's faithful handling of the grain offering produces a soothing, acceptable result before God.
The griddle-baked grain offering is presented as a pleasing aroma — the careful preparation and offering of even a cooked grain portion is designed to evoke a soothing divine reception.
Moses burns the whole ram as a burnt offering with a pleasing aroma — obedient execution of the ordination rite produces the soothing aroma that signals God's full acceptance of the consecration.
The ordination offering burned on the altar produces a pleasing aroma — the soothing quality marks divine acceptance of the priests' inauguration, confirming the rite's effectiveness before God.

Fat burned on the altar at the tent of meeting produces a pleasing aroma to the Lord — proper blood and fat handling at the appointed place generates the soothing divine acceptance the sacrifice requires.
The grain offering accompanying the firstfruits wave is a food offering with a pleasing aroma — the soothing character of this festal offering confirms God's delight in the appointed-feast worship.
The burnt offering of lambs, bull, and rams presented at Pentecost is a food offering with a pleasing aroma — the soothing quality affirms that God receives this full festal presentation with favor.

Israel is commanded to rejoice before the Lord for seven days at the feast — joy here is a prescribed corporate response directed Godward, flowing from festive celebration with branches in God's presence.
God declares he will not smell the pleasing aromas of a disobedient Israel's sanctuaries — divine favor is withdrawn; the soothing effect of sacrifice ceases when the covenant relationship is broken.

The day of gladness, feasts, and new moons are occasions when trumpets sound over offerings — communal joy is given a ritual voice, marking Israel's inner gladness as something to be declared before God.
Moses invites Hobab by promising 'we will do good to you' — the good is oriented outward as benefit and blessing toward another, grounded in God's own promised good to Israel.

The people judge return to Egypt as better than entering Canaan — fear overrides trust, and their inner evaluation of what is preferable is distorted by dread of death rather than faith in God's promise.
If the Lord delights in us he will bring us into the land — divine delight is the inner condition that moves God's will toward fulfilling his promise, making his pleasure the hinge of Israel's fate.

Sacrifices from herd or flock are offered to make a pleasing aroma to the Lord — the stated inner purpose of the offering is to produce a soothing, favorable effect in God, whether vow, freewill, or festal.
The drink offering's wine produces a soothing, pleasing quality that satisfies or appeases the Lord, suggesting the inner disposition of acceptable worship is one that brings restful pleasure to God.
The half-hin wine drink offering creates a soothing aroma to the Lord, indicating that properly measured, willing sacrifice produces a calming, pleasing effect directed toward God.
Every native Israelite's food offering carries a soothing quality before the Lord, marking right worship as that which calms and pleases God through faithful, prescribed obedience.
Even a resident foreigner who wishes to offer to God must present it as a soothing aroma, showing that the inner desire to please God must be expressed through the same approved ritual form.
An unintentional communal sin is remedied by a burnt offering that produces a soothing aroma to the Lord, showing that restoring relational calm with God requires proper atoning sacrifice.
The fat of firstborn animals burned on the altar produces a soothing aroma to the Lord, indicating that offering what is holy and dedicated to God brings a settling, pleasing response from him.

God describes the pleasing aroma offering as 'my food,' something he claims personally, indicating that the soothing quality of these offerings is directed toward satisfying God's own relational appetite.
The regular burnt offering ordained at Sinai is specifically defined as producing a pleasing, soothing aroma, grounding this quality in covenantal institution rather than individual discretion.
The evening lamb offering mirrors the morning's soothing aroma, showing that twice-daily faithful worship sustains a continuous, calming relationship between the worshiper and God.
Fine flour mixed with oil accompanying each lamb produces a soothing aroma, showing that even supporting grain offerings contribute to the overall pleasing, calming effect of worship on God.

Seven consecutive days of food offerings producing a soothing aroma shows that sustained, repeated, obedient worship maintains an ongoing calming and pleasing relationship with God throughout the feast.

The Feast of Weeks burnt offering of bulls, ram, and lambs is specifically presented to produce a soothing aroma, indicating that celebrating abundance before God requires worship that brings him genuine pleasure.
The New Year's burnt offering of unblemished animals is offered for a soothing aroma, linking the solemn start of the seventh month with worship designed to produce calm, settled pleasure in God.
Multiple layers of regular and appointed offerings together produce a soothing aroma, showing that the cumulative, orderly observance of all prescribed worship sustains God's pleased response.
On the Day of Atonement the burnt offering of unblemished animals is a soothing aroma to the Lord, linking the solemn inner posture of national contrition with worship that calms and pleases God.

The Feast of Tabernacles' large burnt offering of thirteen bulls and other animals produces a soothing aroma, showing that the greatest festival requires the most abundant worship to fully satisfy God.

The closing assembly's burnt offering produces a soothing aroma, indicating that even the final, reduced offering of a single bull maintains the same pleasing, calming quality before God as the whole feast.
The daughters of Zelophehad are told to marry whom they think 'best,' locating pleasant or good judgment within personal inner assessment, though constrained by tribal covenant boundaries.

The people's inner agreement that Moses's proposed plan is 'good' reflects a collective inner approval and willingness, showing the pleasant quality as a felt sense of rightness about a course of action.

Doing what is 'right and good' in God's sight is both a moral and relational act that leads to Israel's wellbeing, linking the inner disposition of ethical goodness directly to experienced flourishing.
After eating and being satisfied, Israel is to bless God for the 'good land,' showing that material goodness received from God naturally evokes an inner response of thankful acknowledgment.
God clarifies that the 'good land' is a gift of grace, not earned merit, countering any inner sense of self-righteous entitlement and reframing goodness as belonging to God's character, not Israel's.
Rejoicing before God at the chosen place is commanded as a wholehousehold response to divine blessing, grounding joy in experienced gratitude for what God has provided in all that they undertake.

Rejoicing before God is commanded to include the whole social community, including the landless Levite, showing that this joy is relational and inclusive rather than private or exclusive to the prosperous.
Rejoicing before the Lord in all that one undertakes is tied to eating at the chosen sanctuary place, locating joy in active, engaged worship rather than in personal achievement alone.
Doing what is 'good and right' in God's sight is the condition for multigenerational wellbeing, showing that the inner orientation toward moral goodness produces lasting, observable good outcomes.

Israel is permitted to spend tithe money on whatever their appetite craves and then rejoice before God, placing joy in the context of freely enjoyed abundance in God's presence with household community.

Rejoicing before God at Weeks encompasses every social stratum including servants, Levites, and the vulnerable, showing that this joy is expansive, socially restorative, and directed toward God's dwelling place.

Rejoicing in the feast of Tabernacles is commanded for every household member and marginalized neighbor, showing that Israel's joy is to be shared outwardly and is not confined to the personally fortunate.
God's blessing of produce and labor will result in Israel being 'altogether joyful,' presenting gladness as a comprehensive inner state that overflows from divine blessing during the seven-day feast.

The term 'too difficult' (pa.la) here does not evidence joy or wonder as an inner-being state; rather it describes a legal case that is too complex or extraordinary for local judges, requiring higher adjudication.
Purging innocent blood from the community so 'it may be well with you' frames goodness not as an inner feeling but as a moral-covenantal state of communal wellbeing achieved through just action.
Delight here is presented as a conditional inner disposition — if a man no longer delights in a captive wife, that waning desire creates a moral obligation to release rather than exploit her.

The escaped slave is to settle wherever it 'suits him' (tov, good/pleasant to him), locating the quality of pleasant goodness in the slave's own inner sense of fit and security, not the master's pleasure.
A newly married man is exempted from duty for a year to 'make happy' his wife, showing that rejoicing/joy is a relational act of gladness directed toward another person, requiring time and presence.

A man's refusal to perform levirate duty reflects a lack of inner willingness or delight in marrying his brother's widow, showing that this joy-term here marks the absence of relational desire needed for duty.

The man's persistent 'I do not wish to take her' exposes an inner unwillingness to delight in the required relationship, revealing the term's negative here as a stubborn refusal of covenantal obligation.

Rejoicing in all the good God has given is commanded after presenting firstfruits, grounding joy in a deliberate act of grateful acknowledgment of God's provision shared with the community and sojourner.
After sacrificing peace offerings and eating, Israel is commanded to rejoice before God, locating joy in communal celebration at covenant renewal as a response to enacted worship on Mount Ebal.

Joy (sim.chah) is described as the proper inner disposition for serving God, and its absence — failure to serve with joyfulness and gladness of heart amid abundance — becomes the root cause of coming judgment.

Under siege-curse conditions, even the most tender and refined man — whose comfort-orientation defines him — collapses inward, his former delicacy now transformed into cruel withholding from his own family.

The most delicate and tender woman, so refined she would not touch the ground with her foot, is now driven by siege-desperation to deny food to her own family, showing delight utterly destroyed by judgment.

The 'most tender and refined' (a.nog) woman's characteristic daintiness is presented as her defining inner quality of luxury-softness, which the curse inverts into desperate, grotesque self-preservation.

The term pa.la here describes afflictions as 'extraordinary' or beyond normal — not an inner-being state of wonder but the superlative, overwhelming severity of divine judgment, evoking dread rather than joy.

God's delight in bringing ruin mirrors his former delight in doing Israel good, showing that divine joy can operate in judgment as readily as in blessing, directed by Israel's own covenantal faithfulness or failure.

God's renewed delight in prospering the restored Israel parallels his delight in the fathers, showing that divine pleasure is relational and restorable, grounded in covenantal faithfulness rather than performance alone.
The Lord will 'again take delight' in prospering restored Israel just as he did the patriarchs, locating divine joy in the inner pleasure God takes in blessing those who return to him wholeheartedly.

The commandment is declared 'not too hard' (pa.la / beyond reach), meaning obedience is not an overwhelming or wonder-invoking impossibility but an accessible inner and communal act within human capacity.

Moses blesses Zebulun with a call to rejoice in its seafaring commerce and Issachar in its settled life, showing that joy is tribe-specific, rooted in each group's particular vocation and God-given portion.

The people are called to consecrate themselves in anticipation of wonders the Lord will do, positioning wonder as an expected inner response to extraordinary divine action that demands prior moral preparation.
The Gibeonites submit entirely to Israel's discretion, framing whatever action seems inwardly right and fitting to their captor as the standard by which they should be treated.
The Lord's promises are characterised as 'good'—entirely reliable and beneficial—and the inner assurance is confirmed by their complete fulfilment without a single failure.

God's 'good things' are the pleasant, beneficial blessings he promised; their certain fulfilment serves as the very measure by which the coming evil consequences of disobedience will be equally certain.
The 'good land' is the pleasant, desirable inheritance God gave Israel; covenant faithfulness is what determines whether that inwardly valued gift is retained or lost.
Gideon appeals to the memory of God's wondrous deeds—acts that provoked astonishment and awe in past generations—but feels their absence acutely in his present experience of abandonment.
Gideon uses 'better' to assess comparative worth, arguing that even Ephraim's lesser contribution surpasses his own greater effort, appealing to their inner sense of value to defuse rivalry.
Abimelech frames his sole rule as the more beneficial and preferable option for Shechem's leaders, appealing to their judgment of what is advantageous for them.
In Jotham's parable, wine symbolises the capacity to gladden both God and human beings, portraying joy as a valued gift produced and shared outwardly rather than an inner solitary state.
Mutual rejoicing between Abimelech and Shechem is presented as the fitting inner-relational outcome of genuine good faith and integrity; its absence would expose the hollowness of their alliance.
Israel submits entirely to God's judgment, acknowledging that whatever he inwardly determines as right and fitting is the proper standard for dealing with their sin.
The Lord is identified as 'the one who works wonders,' a designation pointing to acts that exceed ordinary expectation and are witnessed with awe by Manoah and his wife.

Manoah's wife reasons from God's gracious actions that he did not intend their death—his willingness to receive their offering and reveal his word implies inner desire toward their life, not destruction.

The Philistine lords gather to celebrate with communal joy over what they believe is their god Dagon's victory in delivering Samson; their rejoicing is directed outward in festive worship and boasting.
The Danites appeal to the Levite's inner sense of advantage, arguing that priestly service to an entire tribe is a more desirable and beneficial position than serving one household.
The father-in-law's inner delight at the husband's return is expressed in the warmth and eagerness with which he comes out to meet him, showing joy as a relational, welcoming response.
The host uses 'what seems good to you' to defer to the mob's own inclination and judgment, framing their desire as the criterion for action—however morally degraded that standard is.
Naomi judges it genuinely beneficial and wise for Ruth to remain with Boaz's workers, her inner assessment weighing Ruth's safety and welfare as the measure of what is good.
Boaz presents the nearer kinsman's willingness to redeem as the deciding factor; if that man desires to act, good—but if he lacks that inner willingness, Boaz himself will fulfil the obligation.

Boaz uses 'good' as an affirmation of the fitting outcome if the nearer kinsman exercises his right—the word signals inner approval of that proper resolution before offering himself as the alternative.
Ruth's love for Naomi is evaluated as surpassing seven sons—a superlative of relational goodness—showing that inner devotion and affection constitute the highest measure of benefit to another person.
Elkanah asks whether his love for Hannah is not more valuable to her than ten sons, appealing to her inner sense of worth to console her grief—but her heart remains unrelieved.
Elkanah defers to Hannah's own inner judgment on the timing of weaning Samuel, framing her personal discernment as the right guide for domestic decision-making.
Hannah's rejoicing is rooted in her heart's exultation in the Lord who brought her salvation; the joy is constitutionally located in her heart and directed entirely toward God's saving act.
Eli pronounces the report about his sons' behaviour 'not good'—a morally negative inner evaluation—because it dishonours God and damages the community's perception of the Lord's people.
The Lord's will or desire was fixed on bringing Eli's sons to death, indicating that divine inner resolve—here toward judgment—overrides human intercession when wickedness is entrenched.

Eli submits to the Lord's sovereign judgment with the phrase 'let him do what seems good to him,' expressing resigned acceptance of whatever God's inner will determines, even if it means his house's ruin.
The people of Beth-shemesh rejoiced at the sight of the returning ark, their inner gladness welling up spontaneously from the visual recognition of God's presence returning to them.
Saul affirms his servant's suggestion with 'well said,' a brief expression of inner approval indicating that the proposal strikes him as sound and agreeable.
The men of Jabesh-gilead were glad when they heard the promise of deliverance from Saul's army; their gladness is an immediate inner response to the news of imminent salvation.
The men of Jabesh surrender to Nahash, deferring entirely to his judgment by saying 'do whatever seems good to you'—a capitulation framing the enemy's inner will as the standard of action.
All Israel and Saul rejoiced greatly at Gilgal after the victory and coronation; this collective, exuberant inner gladness is directed Godward through peace offerings celebrating his deliverance.
Samuel commits to instructing Israel in 'the good and the right way,' presenting moral goodness as an inner orientation that must be taught and walked in to maintain right relationship with God.
The people defer to Saul's military judgment by saying 'do whatever seems good to you,' placing the determination of right action entirely within his inner assessment of the situation.
The people again submit to Saul's judgment with 'do what seems good to you,' repeatedly affirming his inner discernment as the rightful guide for Israel's course of action.

Samuel challenges whether God's inner delight lies in ritual sacrifice or in obedience; the answer is that God's pleasure is fundamentally oriented toward the willing, hearing heart, not external offerings.
Samuel declares obedience 'better than sacrifice,' establishing that inner compliance with God's word is of greater moral worth than external religious acts performed without that inner submission.
Samuel tells Saul the kingdom is given to one who is 'better than you'—a moral and qualitative judgment that the successor possesses an inner character more fitting for kingship than Saul's.
Being 'well' here describes Saul's restored inner state when the harmful spirit departs; the lyre's soothing effect produces an inward calm that is the positive opposite of spiritual torment.
Saul's refreshed and 'well' condition after David's playing names the positive inner state of relief and calm that came when the harmful spirit left him—inner well-being restored through music.

The women celebrate David's victory with songs of joy and instruments, expressing collective communal gladness that overflows in festive music, dancing, and public celebration directed toward the returning warriors.

Saul's servants relay that the king inwardly delights in David, a claimed affective disposition of favour and pleasure toward him—though the broader context reveals this delight is false and manipulative.
Saul publicly frames his desire as requiring no bride-price except Philistine foreskins, but his stated pleasure conceals an inner murderous intent—desire used as a mask for deadly scheming.
Jonathan's inner delight in David is strong enough to resist his father's murderous command, showing that deep personal affection can override familial and political loyalty when the will is engaged.
Jonathan argues that David's deeds have produced genuine benefit for Saul, appealing to the morally good outcomes of David's loyalty as grounds for sparing his life.
Jonathan reminds Saul that he himself once rejoiced over David's victory, using Saul's past inner gladness as moral evidence against his current murderous intent toward an innocent man.
David and Jonathan use the word 'good' as a signal of Saul's inner disposition—a favourable response meaning Saul holds no harmful intent, while anger would signal determined danger.
Jonathan refers to Saul being 'well disposed' toward David, using the good/pleasant term to describe an inward attitude of favour—the emotional and volitional state Jonathan will probe and report.
Saul acknowledges that David's sparing of him was an act of genuine goodness, and invokes the Lord to repay David with corresponding good—recognising moral worth and expecting its relational reward.

Abigail is described as discerning and beautiful—'good' in understanding—contrasting sharply with Nabal's harsh inner character, showing that goodness here names an inward quality of wisdom and perception.
David's men are described as 'very good' to Nabal's servants during shared time in the fields—their goodness expressed in protective, considerate treatment that caused no harm or loss.
The term appears in negation — the act is declared 'not good,' functioning as a moral judgment that the soldiers' failure to guard the Lord's anointed was inwardly blameworthy and deserving of death.
David reasons within his heart that escaping to Philistine territory is the better option for survival; the inner faculty of deliberate judgment weighs two futures and concludes flight is preferable.
Achish's inner assessment of David is that his conduct is right and trustworthy; the term expresses a favorable moral verdict formed in the will and perception of the one judging.
Achish affirms David as blameless in his own sight, comparing him to an angel of God; this represents an inward estimation of moral character held as self-evident by the speaker.
David's lament suppresses news of defeat lest enemy women break into rejoicing; the inner state of exultant triumph over a fallen foe is what must not be stirred in the Philistines.
Saul and Jonathan are eulogized as 'lovely,' expressing an affective, relational quality that made them beloved and admirable to those around them in life and even in death.
Saul's provision of luxurious scarlet clothing and gold ornaments is the object of the daughters' mourning; delight here is expressed through the sensory pleasure and adornment he gave them.

David grieves that Jonathan's companionship was deeply pleasant to him, surpassing even the love of women; the inner relational quality is one of profound emotional warmth and closeness experienced in the soul.

Jonathan's love is described as 'extraordinary,' a term of wonder marking it as surpassing ordinary human affection; the inner experience of that bond struck David as something beyond the normal range of feeling.
David's single-word assent 'Good' expresses willing inner agreement to the covenant proposed; it reflects a deliberate volitional commitment shaped by his own desire to have Michal returned.
David brings the ark to Jerusalem 'with rejoicing,' an outwardly expressed inner gladness prompted by God's blessing on Obed-edom's household and the sacred presence of the ark.
Joab defers the outcome to whatever 'seems good' to God; the term frames divine will as the sovereign inner determination that governs the outcome of the battle.
Amnon finds it 'impossible' to reach Tamar — the term marks a state of frustrated incapacity that intensifies his inner torment, as the barrier against his desire seems overwhelming.
Absalom's silence — speaking neither good nor bad to Amnon — is explained by deep inner hatred; the absence of pleasant speech signals a heart consumed with unspoken contempt.
Absalom judges it would have been better to remain in exile than to return ignored; his inner evaluation of the two options reveals bitter dissatisfaction with the outcome of his return.
Absalom flatters petitioners by telling them their claims are 'good and right,' appealing to their inner sense of justice to win their loyalty away from David.

David expresses that if God declares 'I have no pleasure in you,' he submits entirely to that divine will; delight here is the determining inner disposition of God toward David that would govern his fate.
Hushai counters Ahithophel by declaring his counsel 'not good,' framing the dispute as a judgment about the quality of strategic thinking offered to Absalom.
Israel's preference for Hushai's counsel over Ahithophel's is described as God's ordained defeat of 'good counsel'; divine will overrides even superior human strategy to bring harm on Absalom.
The troops argue David is worth ten thousand of them, making it 'better' for him to support from the city; this inner calculus of relative worth shapes the collective will about David's role.
The king identifies the approaching runner as a 'good man' who brings good news; moral character is linked to the reliability of the news, reflecting inner trustworthiness perceived by David.
The servants cross the Jordan to do the king's 'pleasure,' indicating that willing service to the king's desire is the inner orientation motivating their action.
Mephibosheth appeals to the king's discernment to 'do what seems good,' entrusting the outcome entirely to the king's inner moral judgment, comparing it to an angel's wisdom.
David pledges to do whatever 'seems good' to Barzillai for Chimham; the term reflects the king's generous inner disposition to act according to the other's desire and his own gracious will.
The soldier's call — 'whoever favors Joab' — invokes inward allegiance and loyalty as the criterion for following; the term names the inner disposition of favor that should drive action.
God rescued David because he 'delighted in' him; this divine inner delight is presented as the motivating cause of rescue, rooted in God's favorable disposition toward David.
David's 'desire' — all my help and my desire — is the object of God's prospering through the everlasting covenant; the term points to David's deepest inner longing secured by divine promise.
Joab challenges why the king should delight in conducting a census; the question probes the inner motivation behind David's desire, implying it is spiritually suspect.
The people's rejoicing at Solomon's anointing is so intense that the earth shook with their noise; gladness erupts as a collective inner response to a momentous public act of kingship.
The people's 'great joy' accompanying Solomon's anointing is so intense it splits the earth; this corporate inner exultation overflows into bodily noise and communal celebration.
Zadok and Nathan and the crowd go up 'rejoicing' after anointing Solomon king; the gladness is a spontaneous inner response to the legitimate transfer of royal authority.
Judah and Israel eat, drink, and are 'happy' under Solomon's prosperous reign; the gladness is a settled inner contentment arising from abundance and peace across the land.
Hiram rejoices greatly upon hearing Solomon's words, blessing God for giving David a wise son; the inner joy is a glad response of the spirit to recognized divine favor and wisdom.
Hiram declares readiness to fulfill 'all you desire' regarding timber; the term names Solomon's inner wish as the directing object that Hiram's will aligns itself to serve.
Hiram asks Solomon to 'meet my wishes' by supplying food; the term frames the inner desire of Hiram as a reciprocal expectation, showing that pleasure functions as a basis for agreement.
Hiram supplies all the timber Solomon 'desired'; the term marks Solomon's inner longing for building materials as the measure that determined the full extent of Hiram's provision.
The people depart 'joyful and glad of heart' after the temple dedication; the inner gladness is explicitly located in the heart and arises from gratitude for all the Lord's goodness shown to Israel.
Solomon builds all that he 'desired to build'; the term captures the inward longing and vision that drove his ambitious construction projects, showing desire as the animating force behind his deeds.
Hiram supplied cedar, cypress, and gold 'as much as he desired'; Solomon's inner appetite for building materials is the measure that determined the full scope of what Hiram provided.
The queen of Sheba blesses God who 'delighted in' Solomon and placed him on Israel's throne; divine delight is the inner affection toward Solomon that motivated God's act of exaltation and kingship.
Solomon gives the queen of Sheba 'all that she desired'; her inner longing, whatever it was, is fully satisfied by the king's generosity, with desire as the measure of his giving.

Whoever 'would' — whoever had the desire — Jeroboam ordained as a high-place priest; the term marks inward willingness or personal inclination as the sole criterion for an office that should require holiness.
Ahab offers Naboth an alternative vineyard 'if it please you'; the term points to Naboth's inner preference or willingness as the condition on which Ahab's proposal rested.

All the people of the land rejoice as the rightful king is crowned; their gladness stands in sharp contrast to Athaliah's horror, marking coronation joy as a collective inner and outward celebration of legitimate rule restored.

All the people of the land rejoice after Athaliah's death and the city is quieted; the rejoicing is a communal inner relief and gladness flowing from the removal of an illegitimate ruler and restoration of order.

Abundant provisions are brought from distant tribes because 'there was joy in Israel'; the joy is a national inner state of celebration that expressed itself in generous, sacrificial communal support.

David appoints Levitical singers to 'raise sounds of joy' with instruments; the joy is an inner exultant gladness that is to be deliberately summoned and expressed through organized, loud musical praise.
David and Israel's leaders bring up the ark 'with rejoicing'; the joy is an inner response to the sacred act of returning God's covenant presence, expressed communally as the procession moves.
Israel is called to tell of God's 'wondrous works'; the inner experience of wonder at divine acts is the motive for proclamation, with God's extraordinary deeds as the object that produces awe.
Those who seek the Lord are called to let their hearts rejoice; the rejoicing is explicitly located in the heart and directed toward God's holy name, arising from the inner posture of seeking him.
The inner act called for is deliberate remembrance of God's wondrous works — memory is the faculty engaged, directed toward past divine miracles and judgments as the object of wonder.
Wonder at God's marvelous works is to be declared outward among all nations — the inner response of awe becomes the impulse driving public proclamation of divine glory.
Joy is named as an attribute dwelling in God's place alongside strength and majesty, situating it as a quality belonging to the divine presence rather than a human emotion in this verse.
The earth's rejoicing is called forth in response to the Lord's reign — the exultation is directed outward as a cosmic reaction to divine sovereignty, grounding the emotion in theological fact.
The heavens' gladness is summoned as the fitting inner-and-expressed response to the Lord's kingship — joy here is the proper relational posture of creation toward its ruling God.

A willing mind is described as the inner disposition that delights in serving God — the faculty is the will oriented wholeheartedly toward God, contrasted implicitly with reluctant or divided devotion.
The people's rejoicing is caused by their own wholehearted freewill giving — joy arises from the inner alignment of the will with generous devotion, and David shares this gladness greatly.
Great joy describes the intensity of David's inner response to witnessing the people's wholehearted freewill offering — it is joy produced by observing devoted generosity directed toward God.
Joy characterizes the inner spirit with which the people freely offer their gifts — it is set alongside uprightness of heart, marking the emotional quality of sincere, uncoerced devotion before God.
Great gladness marks the communal inner state during the feast before the Lord — the joy accompanies worship, eating, and the coronation of Solomon, expressing celebratory fullness of heart.
The term here describes the temple as great and wonderful — it is applied to an object rather than to inner experience, indicating the awe-inspiring quality of what God's house will be.

The saints' rejoicing is to be directed in God's goodness — the inner gladness is oriented specifically toward divine goodness as its source and object, accompanying the priests being clothed with salvation.
Gladness is located in the heart of the people as they return home — the inner state is joy produced by the prosperity God granted, contrasting with any heaviness they might have felt.
God's delight is directed personally toward Solomon — it is the divine inner pleasure that resulted in placing Solomon on the throne, with love for Israel as the underlying motive.
Pleasure here refers to the queen of Sheba's personal desires — the term points to the subjective inner wants that drove her requests, with Solomon granting all she desired.

Rejoicing over the oath is grounded in wholehearted seeking of God — the inner faculty of desire is fully engaged, making the joy an expression of genuine, undivided devotion resulting in God's found presence.
The term here describes Jehoshaphat's heart being courageous or lifted up positively in the ways of the Lord — his inner resolve and elevated spirit are directed toward faithful obedience, not pride.
The Lord is the agent who produced rejoicing in the people — their inner joy over victory against enemies is a gift instilled by God, arising from divine action rather than self-generated emotion.
Joy is the emotional quality of the return journey to Jerusalem — it is divinely caused gladness over triumph against enemies, the dominant inner tone of a community experiencing God's deliverance.

The people's rejoicing accompanies the rightful king's restoration — the glad inner response expressed through trumpets and instruments is contrasted sharply with Athaliah's horror and cry of treason.

Rejoicing accompanies the offering of burnt offerings in the restored temple order — joy is presented as the proper inner spirit of worship, paired with singing as an expression of gladness before God.
The people's rejoicing follows the removal of the usurper Athaliah — their inner gladness is the communal response to restored legitimate rule, paired with the city returning to quietness.

The princes and people rejoiced as they brought their tax offerings — gladness accompanies the act of giving, indicating an inner willingness and positive emotional engagement with contributing to the temple.

The term here describes Uzziah being marvelously helped by God — it points to divine action that inspired wonder in observers, with Uzziah's fame spreading as a result of extraordinary divine assistance.
Uzziah's inner pride grew as he became strong — here the upward movement of the heart turns destructive, with self-exaltation leading him to act unfaithfully against God, resulting in ruin.

Gladness accompanies the singing of praises — it is the inner quality that characterizes the Levites' worship, joined with bowing down, expressing a joyful and reverent spirit in restored temple service.

Hezekiah and the people rejoiced because God had provided suddenly and unexpectedly — their gladness is a response to surprise divine provision, the inner emotion triggered by God's unsolicited initiative.
Great gladness characterizes the feast days — this inner joy is expressed through wholehearted Levitical praise day by day, marking the communal emotional tone of renewed covenant celebration.
Gladness accompanies the assembly's agreement to extend the feast — the inner delight is so strong that the people willingly prolonged their celebration, indicating joy as the motivating inner state.

The entire mixed assembly — priests, Levites, Israelites, and sojourners — shared in rejoicing, indicating that the inner gladness of this Passover celebration extended broadly across all participants.

Great joy in Jerusalem is marked as historically unprecedented since Solomon — the inner communal gladness is measured by its exceptional intensity, pointing to a depth of celebration without recent parallel.

Hezekiah's heart was proud rather than grateful — inner self-exaltation displaced proper thankfulness for divine benefit, and this misdirected elevation of self brought divine wrath on him and his people.

God's goodness is declared as the ground of corporate praise and thanksgiving — the inner recognition of divine goodness and enduring love produces the wholehearted shout of worship at the temple's foundation.
Joy expressed as a loud shout contrasts with the weeping of the elders — the inner gladness of many at the temple's refoundation was so intense it mingled audibly and indistinguishably with grief.

The joyful shout was indistinguishable from weeping — indicating that the inner gladness of some and the grief of others were simultaneously and equally intense, both expressed in loud communal sound.

Joy marks the inner spirit of the returned exiles as they celebrate the temple dedication — it is the communal emotional response to the completion of the house of God after years of exile and effort.
The Lord made the people joyful — their gladness during the Feast of Unleavened Bread is divinely produced, arising from God turning the heart of a foreign king to support them in his work.

Joy is the inner quality with which the feast was kept — it is God-given gladness, named alongside the divine action that turned a foreign king's heart, grounding the emotion in experienced divine favor.

The good hand of God is the enabling presence behind Ezra's successful journey — inner confidence in divine favor is implied, with God's goodness experienced as protective guidance from Babylonia to Jerusalem.

The good hand of God is credited as the source of providential provision — the inner acknowledgment of divine favor shapes gratitude as the community recognizes God's direct agency in supplying capable personnel.

Delight in fearing God's name is the inner disposition of the servants for whom Nehemiah prays — their willing orientation toward reverential obedience is what marks them as those who truly delight in God.

Pleasing the king is the condition for Nehemiah's request — the term points to the king's inner favorable disposition as the gate through which Nehemiah's desire for Jerusalem's restoration must pass.

Again pleasing the king is the stated condition for the request — Nehemiah frames his petition around the king's inner approval as the necessary relational prerequisite for gaining letters of passage.

The good hand of God upon Nehemiah is credited for the king granting his requests — the inner confidence and outcome both trace back to divine favor, with God's goodness as the ultimate enabling force.
The good hand of God upon Nehemiah for good becomes the motivating testimony that stirs the people's inner resolve — hearing of divine favor produces collective willingness to rise up and build.
The thing being done is declared not good — here the term marks a moral deficit, with the people's conduct failing the inner standard of walking in the fear of God and producing shame before enemies.

The joy of the Lord is declared the source of the people's strength — it is positioned as the inner resource that replaces grief, grounding resilience not in self-effort but in God-given gladness on a holy day.

Great rejoicing arises because the people understood the words declared to them — the inner joy is caused by comprehension, making it an intellectual-emotional response to received and grasped divine instruction.

Very great rejoicing accompanies the unprecedented keeping of the Feast of Booths — the intensity of the inner gladness is marked by historical comparison, signaling a joyful recovery of lost communal worship.

Good statutes and commandments given at Sinai are described as coming from God — here goodness marks the moral quality and beneficial character of divine law, implying inner rightness and trustworthiness.

The people's inner being failed to register God's wonders, leaving them unmoved and unresponsive; their lack of wonder hardened into stiff-necked rebellion, the opposite of the awe such acts should have produced.

God's Spirit is described as 'good,' indicating its quality is wholesome and beneficial to the recipients, instructing them inwardly—goodness here characterizes the Spirit's nature and effect on those it guides.
Gladness is the inner animating quality of the dedication celebration, expressed outwardly through thanksgiving and music; it is the emotional heart of the communal worship event.

The rejoicing is God-produced and pervasive—it fills the entire community including women and children—and is so intense it carries outward as sound heard far away, showing its deep communal and inner force.

This 'great joy' is explicitly attributed to God as its source; the inner experience of joy is not self-generated but divinely given, and its greatness matches the greatness of the sacrifices offered.
Judah's joy is directed specifically toward the priests and Levites who minister; the inner delight is relational and institutional, arising from gratitude for faithful priestly service.
Here 'good' functions as a standard of royal approval or pleasure—what pleases or seems fitting to the king's will; it reflects a volitional inner assessment rather than emotional delight.

The king's delight in a woman is a volitional inner disposition that determines her access to him; the concubine's fate depends entirely on whether she captures this royal pleasure and is summoned again.

The king grants Haman full discretion to act 'as seems good to you,' delegating his own inner sense of what is fitting; personal will and judgment are the operative inner faculties here.
Esther frames her request conditionally on what pleases the king, acknowledging that his inner approval is the threshold that must be met before any action can proceed.

Esther again grounds her petition in the king's pleasure, linking his inner approval to the granting of her wish; the repeated formula shows pleasure as the governing inner criterion for royal decision.

Haman's gladness is a heart-state explicitly named—'glad of heart'—but it is immediately contrasted with the rage that replaces it when he sees Mordecai, showing how fragile this inner joy is when pride is threatened.
Haman is advised to go 'joyfully' to the feast after arranging Mordecai's execution; the gladness is anticipatory and contingent on the removal of the rival who disturbs his inner satisfaction.
The king's delight to honor someone is an inner disposition of will and favor; Haman's self-directed assumption reveals that delight implies the highest personal esteem in the king's heart.
The repeated phrase 'the man whom the king delights to honor' marks royal delight as the inner basis and motivation for public bestowing of honor; will and favor drive the outward act.
Royal delight to honor is the inner criterion that determines who receives the elaborate public ceremony; the prescribed actions all flow from and express this prior inner disposition of the king.

The proclamation enacts what the king delights in, making visible the inner royal pleasure; Haman must publicly honor Mordecai as the object of the very delight Haman had assumed was directed at himself.
Esther's petition is grounded in the king's pleasure as the ultimate inner authority; his favorable disposition is the condition on which her life and her people's lives depend.

Multiple conditions of royal pleasure and personal favor are stacked together, showing that inner approval at every level—king's will, sight, and judgment—must align before Esther's request can succeed.
The Jews are granted freedom to write 'as you please,' transferring inner discretion and volitional authority to them; what seems good to their own judgment becomes the operative guide.
The city of Susa's rejoicing is a spontaneous communal outburst at Mordecai's visible elevation; the inner joy erupts into public shouting, showing the emotional impact of the reversal of fortune.
Joy is listed among four gifts the Jews received—light, gladness, joy, and honor—framing it as a concrete inner blessing that accompanies their deliverance and new social standing.
Rejoicing is paired with gladness, joy, and honor as one of the experiential states that filled the Jews after the edict of deliverance; it marks the inner celebratory response to rescue.
Joy spreads geographically wherever the king's edict reaches, showing that the inner state is triggered by news of deliverance; it manifests communally as feasting and holiday observance.

Rejoicing accompanies gladness as the inner response to the reversal of the Jews' threatened fate; the pairing intensifies the picture of emotional relief and celebration spreading through every province.
Esther again conditions her request on the king's pleasure, confirming that his inner approval is the only basis on which she ventures to ask for further action against her enemies.

Gladness is the inner quality that defines the fourteenth day, institutionalizing the emotional response to deliverance into a recurring day of feasting; relief and joy become commemorated experience.

For the Jews of Susa who fought an extra day, their gladness is likewise expressed in feasting on the fifteenth day; the inner celebration is timed to follow their specific experience of rest after conflict.

The rural Jews' gladness is expressed through feasting, holiday, and sharing food with one another; the inner joy overflows into generous communal activity, marking the day as defined by delight in fellowship.

Gladness is the inner transformation that replaced sorrow—an explicit emotional reversal from mourning—and is institutionalized as the defining inner quality of Purim, expressed through feasting and generosity.

Job curses the night of his birth by wishing it barred from rejoicing among the calendar days; the absence of any celebratory inner quality is the desired curse, making joylessness the fate of that night.

Job wishes the night of his birth to be barren of any joyful cry; triumphing or jubilant sound is the inner-to-outer expression of life's celebration, and its total exclusion is the curse he lays on that night.

The sufferers Job describes rejoice exceedingly at finding the grave; their inner state is a perverse exultation directed toward death as relief, showing how extreme anguish can invert the normal objects of joy.

Those in unbearable suffering are glad when they find the grave; this inner gladness directed at death reveals suffering so severe that the normal orientation of joy is completely reversed toward annihilation.

These sufferers rejoice and are glad specifically at the prospect of the grave; the inner gladness arises from the anticipation of death as release, depicting an extreme inversion of normal joyful inner states.

God's marvelous deeds are described as beyond number and unsearchable, positioning wonder as a response to a reality that exceeds human comprehension; the inner capacity for wonder is overwhelmed by infinite divine acts.

Job finds a source of inner exultation even in unsparing pain—his faithfulness to God's word; the joy does not come from comfort but from moral integrity, showing conscience and will as the seat of this rejoicing.

Bildad sardonically calls the godless man's prosperity 'the joy of his way,' implying a shallow inner delight that vanishes as quickly as the man himself, leaving only others to spring up in his place.

Job uses 'wish' to express an inner desire to contend with God, but frames it as futile; the delight or desire of the will is real but is crushed by the impossibility of answering God even once in a thousand times.

God's marvelous works are beyond number and beyond searching out; wonder is implied as the only fitting inner response to a reality that exceeds all human investigation, leaving the mind in perpetual incomprehension.

Job considers the inner act of being cheerful as something he could attempt by an act of will—putting off his sad face—but the surrounding context makes clear he cannot sustain it; cheerfulness is a desired but unattainable inner posture.

Job challenges God by asking whether it truly seems 'good' to God to oppress him; the term marks the inner moral judgment or pleasure of God, and Job implies that oppressing his own creature cannot genuinely satisfy divine goodness.

Job laments that any sign of recovery would provoke God to hunt him again and work wonders against him; here wonders are experienced not as cause for inner awe and delight but as terrifying divine power directed in judgment.

Job pleads for a brief respite from suffering so he might find a little cheer before death; the inner state of cheerfulness is presented as minimal and temporary, a small relief he can barely hope for amid overwhelming grief.

Job desires intensely to argue his case before the Almighty himself rather than with his friends; this inner longing or delight is directed toward direct encounter with God, reflecting an urgent will to be heard and vindicated.

Job asks whether it will be 'well' for his friends when God searches them out; the term points to whether their inner state and actions will prove satisfactory under divine scrutiny, implying that feigned goodness cannot deceive God.

The triumphing or exulting of the wicked is explicitly declared brief; the inner experience of jubilant joy for the godless is fleeting and momentary, its transience being the moral point Zophar makes about ill-gotten prosperity.
The joy of the godless lasts only a moment; this inner state, though real, is radically temporary, and its brevity reveals that joy built on wickedness has no lasting inner foundation.

The wicked man gets no enjoyment from his trading profits—they must be returned; the inner satisfaction or pleasure of possessing ill-gained wealth is denied him, leaving toil without any inner reward.

Rejoicing here is expressed through music and festivity; the wicked find inner gladness through communal celebration with instruments, an outward expression of their untroubled, God-ignoring enjoyment of life.

The wicked have no inner desire for God's ways; they actively refuse knowledge of him, revealing that delight is entirely absent from their will toward God — their inner orientation is one of rejection.
The wicked feel no inner concern for those who follow them after death; pleasure in legacy or family is absent since their months are cut off, exposing how self-limited their inner investment is.

Eliphaz questions whether God derives any inner pleasure from human righteousness, implying that divine delight cannot be earned or produced by human moral performance — God's pleasure is not contingent on human merit.
The righteous feel inward gladness when they witness the downfall of the wicked, a satisfaction rooted in the vindication of moral order and God's justice rather than mere schadenfreude.

Delight in the Almighty is presented as a future inner state the restored person will experience — a joyful intimacy with God expressed by lifting one's face to him, the inner being oriented freely toward the divine.

The hypocrite's inner being is probed: true delight in the Almighty is linked to persistent prayer, and the implied answer is no — the godless person has no genuine inward pleasure in God and therefore will not call on him.

Pleasure here belongs to the poor — their desire for basic provision; Job defends himself against withholding what the poor longed for, showing that inner want or desire is the frame of reference for his ethical self-examination.

Job denies having found inner rejoicing in material wealth or the fruit of his own labor; he presents misplaced joy in riches as a moral failure he has avoided, revealing that the heart's exultation must not be rooted in possessions.

Job denies taking inner delight in an enemy's ruin; he treats rejoicing over another's calamity as a moral wrong he has not committed, showing that such inward gladness at harm is ethically reprehensible.
Elihu's desire here is directed toward Job's justification rather than his condemnation; his inner inclination is to see Job vindicated, and he invites Job to speak so that desire can be fulfilled.

The 'good' here is the object of communal moral discernment — what is right and truly good is to be chosen collectively, suggesting the inner faculty of moral judgment is engaged in seeking what is genuinely beneficial and right.

Exaltation here is the condition God grants to the righteous — they are set with kings on thrones forever; this is an elevation of status and honor that God bestows, reflecting divine approval lifting the inner and outer standing of the just.

Pleasantness describes the quality of life — peaceful, satisfying years — that comes when one obeys and serves God; it is the inner and experiential fruit of responsive obedience, contrasted implicitly with affliction.
God's thunderous voice produces wonder by doing things beyond human comprehension; the inner response called for is awe before divine acts that exceed the limits of human understanding and knowledge.

Job is called to stop and inwardly consider God's wondrous works; the command engages the faculty of contemplative attention, directing wonder toward what God does rather than toward human circumstance.

The wondrous works of God — specifically the balancing of clouds — exceed Job's knowledge; wonder is provoked by encountering divine perfection in knowledge that surpasses human comprehension of natural phenomena.

Job recognizes he spoke of things too wonderful for him — beyond his grasp; wonder here marks the boundary of human understanding, and Job's inner response is humility before the incomprehensible nature of God's ways.
Delight is the inner orientation of the blessed man toward God's law — not duty but pleasure — expressed in constant meditation day and night; the law becomes the heart's chosen object of joy.
Rejoicing is commanded in combination with fear and trembling, showing that joy before God is not carefree but reverent — the inner being exults within the bounds of awe, not in place of it.

God places joy directly into the heart — the deepest seat of inner life — and this joy surpasses the happiness others find in material abundance; it is a God-given inner gladness, not circumstantially produced.

God's inner nature is defined negatively here: he takes no delight in wickedness and cannot coexist with evil; divine pleasure is constitutionally opposed to moral evil, revealing the holy character of God's will.

Rejoicing flows from taking refuge in God — it is the inward gladness of those who trust him, sustained and expressed in singing; the object of delight is God's name and his protective presence over his people.

The psalmist recounts God's wonderful deeds with his whole heart, linking wonder to wholehearted thanksgiving; the inner faculty of remembrance and praise is fully engaged in response to what God has done.
The psalmist's rejoicing is directed entirely toward God — gladness and exultation are responses to who God is, expressed in praise of his name; joy here is relational and God-directed at its core.

Rejoicing in God's salvation is the goal toward which the psalmist's hope is directed; it is an anticipated inner exultation that will be publicly expressed when God delivers, rooted in trust in his saving acts.

The enemy's potential rejoicing over the psalmist's defeat is the feared outcome; this enemy joy would be triumphant and gloating, the inner gladness of those who prevail over the righteous — the opposite of what the psalmist prays for.

The heart is the explicit seat of rejoicing here, and it rejoices in God's salvation in response to trust in his steadfast love; inner joy is grounded in faithful relationship with God, not in outward circumstances.

Jacob's rejoicing is the anticipated communal inner gladness that will erupt when God restores his people from captivity; the joy is eschatological in orientation, triggered by God's saving action on behalf of Israel.
Israel's gladness is the companion response to Jacob's rejoicing — a national inner joy at the restoration of God's people, awaited with longing and rooted in hope for divine salvation from Zion.

The psalmist's delight resides entirely in the community of the faithful — the saints are the object of all his inner pleasure; relational belonging to the people of God is itself the source of deep personal joy.

Pleasant places describe the inheritance God has measured out for the psalmist; the sense of inner contentment and satisfaction with one's lot is rooted in trust that God has given a good and beautiful portion.
The whole being — heart and inner self — rejoices, and this gladness extends even to physical security; rejoicing here flows from security in God and pervades the entire person, inner and outer.

The heart is the named seat of gladness, and rejoicing begins there before extending to the whole being; the inner heart's gladness is the foundation from which full personal joy and bodily security flow.

At God's right hand are pleasures forevermore — eternal inner delight experienced in his presence; the pleasant things here are not earthly comforts but the enduring joy that comes from dwelling with God himself.

Fullness of joy is found in God's presence — not partial or temporary gladness but complete joy; the inner being reaches its maximum capacity for delight only in the direct experience of God's nearness.

God's delight in the psalmist is the motivating cause of his rescue; divine inner pleasure in the person becomes the basis of saving action, making God's delight both relational and redemptively operative.

The sun's course is compared to a strong man running with joy; the image projects vigorous, exuberant inner gladness onto the sun's movement, capturing the quality of energetic delight in purposeful action.

The Lord's precepts actively cause the heart to rejoice — the inner faculty of emotion is directly affected by God's word; joy is not self-generated but produced in the heart by contact with divine instruction.

The king exults greatly in God's salvation; this exultation is the inner overflow of gladness at what God has accomplished, directed entirely toward God's saving power rather than any human achievement.

The king's rejoicing is grounded in God's strength — his inner gladness is sourced in divine power, not personal capability; rejoicing here names the heart's response to God's might working on the king's behalf.

God makes the king glad with the joy of his own presence; gladness is produced by divine nearness and is itself a gift God grants — the inner state of delight flows directly from being in God's company.
The joy of God's presence is the specific content of the king's gladness; this joy is relational — it is the inner delight of being before God — and it is given by God as an enduring blessing.

The mockers claim God will deliver if he truly delights in the sufferer; divine delight is invoked as the criterion for saving action — if God takes inner pleasure in this person, then rescue should follow.

God's goodness here is a character attribute — he is good and upright — that expresses itself in instructing sinners; the pleasantness of God's nature is morally oriented, directing others toward right paths.

The psalmist publicly tells of God's wondrous deeds as an act of thanksgiving; wonder at what God has done becomes the substance of public praise, with the inner sense of amazement expressed outwardly in proclamation.

Gazing on the beauty of the Lord in his temple is the one supreme desire; pleasantness describes the Lord's own being — his beauty is the object of the psalmist's deepest longing and the goal of his inner seeking.
The psalmist prays that enemies not rejoice over him; enemy rejoicing would be triumphant gloating at his downfall — a joy rooted in rivalry and hostility, which God's deliverance has prevented.

God transforms mourning into dancing and clothes the psalmist with gladness; joy is a divine gift replacing grief — the inner state is entirely reversed by God's action, making gladness a garment God himself puts on the person.

The psalmist's rejoicing is grounded in God's steadfast love and his perception of the psalmist's affliction; inner gladness flows from the felt confidence that God has seen and known the soul's distress and acts in faithful love.

The psalmist's rejoicing is directed toward God's steadfast love, grounded in the assurance that God has seen his affliction and known the distress of his soul — making joy a response to felt divine knowledge of inner suffering.

Wonder arises in the psalmist as a response to God's extraordinary display of steadfast love during extreme danger (a besieged city), making the feeling an inner acknowledgment of the surpassing nature of divine faithfulness.
Rejoicing is commanded to the righteous and upright in heart, locating this joy expressly within the heart as its seat, directed toward the Lord as its object and expressed outwardly in a shout.
Gladness is commanded to the righteous and directed toward the Lord, with uprightness of heart named as the characteristic of those called to this inward delight expressed in vocal celebration.
The heart is explicitly named as the site where gladness resides, and trust in God's holy name is given as its cause — making joy the inner fruit of confident reliance on God.
The humble are invited to be glad upon hearing the soul's boast in the Lord, so gladness here is an inward response within lowly listeners to news of God's goodness proclaimed by another.

Delighting here is expressed as a deep desire — the man who 'desires life' — showing that the inner faculty of longing drives the pursuit of good days, making delight the motivational root of life's direction.
The soul itself is the subject of rejoicing, directed toward the Lord and expressed as exultation in his salvation, placing this joy at the deepest personal level in response to divine rescue.
The soul exults in God's salvation, with this rejoicing paired with the soul's earlier-named rejoicing in the Lord — both terms together depict the soul's total inner elation at divine deliverance.
Here rejoicing is the enemies' malicious inner pleasure at the psalmist's stumbling — a perverse delight that turns others' misfortune into a cause for gathering against him.
The psalmist pleads that enemies not be allowed to rejoice over him, identifying such rejoicing as the triumphant inner satisfaction of wrongful foes — a gloating pleasure opposed to justice.
The psalmist appeals to God's righteousness to prevent enemies from rejoicing over him, casting their potential rejoicing as a morally wrongful inner triumph that divine vindication should deny.

Being glad at another's calamity is here treated as a morally culpable inner state deserving shame — gladness directed at an innocent person's ruin is condemned as the structural opposite of righteous joy.
Delighting in the psalmist's righteousness is the inner disposition that prompts its bearers to shout for joy, showing delight as the motivating inner orientation that issues in glad, vocal praise.

Those who delight in righteousness are called to rejoice and be glad, so gladness here is the inward companion of delight in what is right, expressed in ongoing verbal praise of God's care for his servant.

Pleasant/good is here its negative — the wicked man chooses a way that is 'not good,' showing that the pleasant or good is what he has deliberately rejected, defining the absence of moral pleasantness as a willed inner posture.
God's 'river of delights' is the source from which worshippers drink, making delight something lavishly supplied by God himself — an overwhelming pleasure experienced by those who abide in his house.

Delighting in the Lord is an inner orientation of the whole self directed toward God, and it is causally linked to God granting the heart's desires — delight in God aligns the will with his own giving.
The meek delight in abundant peace as their inheritance, so delight here is the inner satisfaction of a quiet soul resting in the security and wholeness that God's provision of peace brings.

Pleasant/better is a comparative moral and experiential judgment — the little owned by the righteous surpasses the abundance of the wicked, redefining what is truly good by the person's character rather than their wealth.
The Lord delights in a person's way, and this divine inner pleasure is what establishes that person's steps — God's delight is the operative cause behind the security and direction of a human life.
The psalmist fears the enemies' rejoicing when he stumbles — their anticipated gloating over his misfortune is portrayed as a malicious pleasure he dreads as a further injury added to his suffering.

Good here is the moral quality the psalmist pursues, for which he is repaid with evil — the pleasant/good is what his will is oriented toward even as others oppose him, defining his inner integrity against their hostility.

Cheerfulness here is a fragile, fleeting brightness the psalmist hopes to recover before death — he asks God to look away so he might 'smile again,' making inner cheer the last glimmer of vitality before departure.

Wonder is evoked by God's multiplied wondrous deeds and thoughts toward humanity — they exceed all comparison and all telling, so the inner response is an overwhelmed astonishment that exceeds the capacity of words.
God does not delight in sacrifice and burnt offering but instead gave an open ear, revealing that his inner pleasure is directed not toward ritual acts but toward attentive, obedient listening.

The psalmist delights to do God's will, and God's law is within his heart — delight here is an inward, heartfelt willingness that makes obedience not duty but desire, locating the characteristic in the heart.
Some take delight in the psalmist's hurt — a malicious inner pleasure in his harm — which he prays will bring them dishonor, treating this destructive delight as a moral wrong deserving judgment.
Those who seek God are called to rejoice and be glad in him, making this exultation the inner response of those whose will is oriented toward God — joy flows from seeking and finding him.
Gladness is the inner state of all who seek God, paired with rejoicing and rooted in him — it issues in the ongoing confession 'Great is the Lord,' linking inner delight to outward proclamation.
The psalmist knows God delights in him because his enemy has not triumphed — God's inner pleasure in the psalmist is evidenced by protective action, making delight a relational, protecting affection.

God himself is called 'my exceeding joy' — the rejoicing is not merely felt before God but found in God as its very substance, making the altar-approach an approach to the source of the psalmist's deepest gladness.

Joy is predicated of God himself as the psalmist's 'exceeding joy,' so the inner experience of joy is inseparable from the person of God — the soul's highest pleasure is God personally, not merely his gifts.

The heart overflows with a pleasing theme — the pleasant here describes the inner quality of the poetic inspiration that wells up from the heart and flows into praise of the king, making pleasantness the felt character of the creative impulse.
Gladness is the substance of the oil with which God anoints the king above his companions — an exaltation given because of the king's love of righteousness, making joy the reward of moral character.

Gladness is paired with joy as the double inner state of those led into the king's palace, making the entrance into royal presence the occasion for a full, joyful gladness that defines the experience of belonging to the king.
River streams make the city of God glad — gladness is the inner effect produced in God's holy city by the life-giving flow that sustains it, linking joy to the divine presence dwelling there.

Mount Zion is called 'the joy of all the earth,' making rejoicing not merely a human inner state but the very identity of the holy city — the whole earth finds its focal point of delight in Zion's beauty and the great King it houses.

The daughters of Judah are called to rejoice because of God's judgments — the rejoicing is directed outward toward divine acts of justice, showing that joy here is the inner response of the community to God's righteous governance.

Mount Zion is called to be glad because of God's judgments, pairing this gladness with the daughters of Judah's rejoicing — together they express a communal inner delight in God's saving justice on behalf of his city.

God delights in truth located in the 'inward being' and wisdom in the 'secret heart,' placing his inner pleasure precisely within the deepest, most hidden part of the person — delight here is God's approval of interior integrity.

The broken bones — a felt, bodily image of inner anguish — are asked to rejoice, so the rejoicing sought here is a restoration of inner vitality and gladness to a self crushed by guilt and divine discipline.
Joy is what the psalmist longs to hear again, paired with gladness, after the brokenness of sin and discipline — it is an inner aliveness whose absence defines the depth of his spiritual suffering.

Rejoicing is the first of two things the psalmist asks to hear, paired with gladness, as the inner sound of restoration — its absence signals a soul silenced by brokenness, and its return would signal healing.

The joy of salvation is something that can be lost and must be restored — it is the inner gladness tied to the experience of being saved, and its recovery is inseparable from a renewed, willing spirit.

God takes no delight in sacrifice — his inner pleasure is not engaged by ritual offerings, implying that what God truly desires lies in the inward disposition of the offerer rather than the external act.

God will delight in right sacrifices when the heart is broken and restored — his pleasure in offerings is conditional on the prior renewal of the inner person, making ritual delight the fruit of inward transformation.

God's name is described as good in the presence of the godly, making pleasantness/goodness the felt quality of God's character experienced within the community of the faithful — it evokes gratitude and waiting trust.

Jacob's inner rejoicing is the anticipated emotional response to God's act of restoring Israel's fortunes — the joy is future, conditional, and collectively directed toward divine salvation from Zion.

Israel's gladness is the inward counterpart to Jacob's rejoicing, both contingent on God's restoration — the whole people's inner being is oriented toward a joy not yet received but earnestly desired.
The Lord's name is called 'good' as the ground for thanksgiving; the pleasantness here is attributed to God's character, which evokes the worshipper's freewill offering and praise.
The righteous person's rejoicing is triggered by witnessing divine justice executed on the wicked — the inner gladness is directed toward the vindication of moral order.
God's steadfast love is evaluated as surpassing even life itself in worth — this superlative pleasantness or goodness becomes the motivating ground for the psalmist's praise.
Triumphing joy rises from the soul's deep satisfaction in God, expressed outwardly through joyful lips — the inner fullness is likened to the richest of foods, centering joy in the soul.
