The king's rejoicing is directed specifically 'in God,' making God himself the object and source of the inner gladness, contrasted implicitly with the silenced lies of his enemies.
The righteous are called to rejoice in the Lord and take refuge in him — the inner gladness and the act of trust are linked, both directed toward God as their object and security.
Joy is attributed to the hills of the wilderness in a vivid personification — the created landscape itself is pictured as girded with rejoicing, expressing the overflowing delight of God's provision.
The people's rejoicing is rooted in the memory of God's mighty act of parting waters — the joy is retrospective, located in a historical encounter with God's saving power at the crossing.
The nations are called to inner gladness because God judges with equity and guides them — the joy is grounded in the perception of divine justice and governance over all peoples.

The righteous are 'jubilant with joy,' the noun joy marking the fullness of their inner exultation before God — it is the culminating quality of their gladness and exulting, all oriented toward God's presence.
The righteous shall be glad as the first in a cascade of inner responses — gladness, exulting, jubilation — all arising in God's presence and set in contrast to the fate of the wicked.
Joy is the stated inner quality characterizing the righteous as jubilant before God — it names the emotional fullness that accompanies their gladness and exulting in the divine presence.
Delight here is directed toward war — certain peoples take inner pleasure in conflict, and the psalmist asks God to scatter them; the delight is morally negative, an appetite for violence.
God's steadfast love is called 'good' as the basis for the psalmist's plea for an answer — the pleasantness of divine love is what gives the petitioner confidence to ask for mercy.
The humble will be glad when they witness God's response — their inner gladness is the natural result of seeing divine action on behalf of the afflicted, reviving the hearts of those who seek God.
Enemies delight inwardly in the psalmist's harm — this perverse pleasure in another's hurt is the inner disposition that warrants their dishonor and shame before God.
Those who seek God are called to rejoice and be glad 'in him' — the joy is directed toward God himself, inseparable from the act of seeking, and expressed in the proclamation of his greatness.
Being glad 'in you' indicates that God is the object and locus of this gladness — the inner rejoicing of God's seekers is sourced in God himself and in love for his salvation.
God's wondrous deeds have been taught to the psalmist from youth and continue to be proclaimed — the wonder resides in the extraordinary acts of God that inspire a lifelong response of declaration.
The Lord alone does wondrous things, making wonder an exclusive attribute of divine action — the inner response implied is awe and worship expressed through the blessing of God's name.
God's goodness is affirmed as directed toward Israel, especially the pure in heart — pleasantness/goodness is a relational quality linking God's character to those whose inner being is clean.
The psalmist desires nothing in all of earth and heaven except God — this exclusive delight centers the will entirely on God, making him the sole object of inner longing and satisfaction.
The community recounts God's wondrous deeds as an act of thanksgiving — wonder at God's nearness and acts prompts and shapes the communal response of praise and gratitude.
The psalmist deliberately calls to memory the Lord's wonders of old as a spiritual exercise — recalling wonder-acts is the inner strategy for sustaining faith through present distress.
God is identified as the one who works wonders, making wonder a defining characteristic of his power — his mighty deeds made known among the peoples inspire awe that points back to God alone.
God's wondrous deeds are to be passed on to the next generation — the wonder is not merely cognitive but formative, shaping the inner life of successive generations through retold memory.
Israel forgot God's wonders — the failure of inner wonder is here a moral failure, as forgetting what God showed them led to unfaithfulness; wonder's absence is the opposite of faithful memory.
God performed wonders before the fathers in Egypt — these acts are publicly witnessed marvels that were meant to embed awe of God's power in the collective memory of his people.
Despite witnessing God's wonders, Israel still sinned and did not believe — the failure of wonder to produce faith shows that wonder must engage the will, not just the senses, to transform behavior.
A single day in God's courts is better than a thousand elsewhere — the pleasantness of God's presence is so surpassing that even the humblest role there outweighs dwelling in wickedness.
The people's rejoicing in God is presented as the purpose of divine revival — God's renewing work aims at producing inner gladness, making joy the telos of restoration.
The psalmist asks God to gladden his soul — joy is located explicitly in the soul, directed toward God to whom the soul is lifted, making God both the agent and the object of inner gladness.
God's goodness is paired with his forgiving nature and abundant steadfast love — pleasantness here is a composite of moral character traits that draw all who call upon him into relationship.
God's wondrous deeds are the evidence and expression of his greatness and uniqueness as God — wonder marks his actions as incomparable, producing awe that recognizes his sole divine status.

The psalmist questions whether God works wonders for the dead — wonder here functions as a marker of living relationship with God; its absence among the dead underscores the urgency of the plea for rescue.
God's wonders are asked if they are known in darkness and forgetfulness — the rhetorical question implies that wonder requires conscious awareness; the dead cannot perceive or declare them.
The heavens themselves are called to praise God's wonders alongside his faithfulness — wonder at God's acts is a fitting response even for the heavenly assembly, linking awe to worship.
The people exult in God's name all day long — the rejoicing is sustained, continuous, and grounded in God's name and righteousness, marking an inner orientation of perpetual delight in God.
God has caused the enemies of his anointed to rejoice — here rejoicing is the inner triumph of adversaries over the fallen king, making it a sign of judgment rather than blessing.
Rejoicing is the desired outcome of being satisfied with God's steadfast love in the morning — the inner joy is dependent on divine love filling the soul, sustaining gladness through all one's days.
Gladness is asked for as a counterbalance to affliction and evil — the inner joy sought from God is proportional to the suffering endured, reflecting a desire for emotional restoration and equity.
The pleasantness (favor) of the Lord is asked to rest upon the community — this divine pleasantness is an outward-flowing grace that confirms and establishes the work of human hands.
It is called 'good' to give thanks and sing praises to God — the pleasantness here is the fittingness and inherent worth of worship, presenting praise as both appropriate and satisfying.
God's work makes the psalmist glad — the inner joy is a direct response to divine action, produced by God himself and expressed outwardly in singing, locating the source of gladness entirely in God.
When the heart is overwhelmed with cares, God's consolations delight the soul — the inner delight is the relief and comfort that God's presence brings, directly countering anxiety within the heart.
God's marvelous works are to be declared among the nations — wonder at his deeds is meant to spread outward through proclamation, making it a missional impulse arising from inner awe.
The earth is called to rejoice alongside the heavens — joy here is cosmic in scope, the whole created order summoned to an inner-like exultation in response to the Lord's reign.

The heavens are to be glad while the earth rejoices — the two terms together describe a universal jubilation, with gladness attributed to the heavens as the inner disposition accompanying cosmic praise.
The Lord's sovereignty over the earth is the direct cause of rejoicing — cosmic kingship calls forth a glad, outward response from the whole earth and its distant shores.
The coastlands are summoned to be glad in response to the Lord's reign — gladness here is the fitting inner and outward reaction of all peoples to divine kingship.
The daughters of Judah rejoice specifically because of God's just judgments — the inner delight is directed outward toward God's moral governance as its cause and object.
Zion hears news of God's judgments and becomes glad — the inner joy is triggered by hearing, connecting the perceptive faculty directly to the emotional response.
Joy is portrayed as something sown and destined to grow for those who are upright in heart — locating it in the heart and linking it to moral integrity as the soil in which it takes root.
The righteous are commanded to direct their rejoicing specifically toward the Lord — the inner gladness is oriented by will and devotion toward God's holy name as its object.
God's marvelous works — salvation wrought by his own hand and arm — are the basis for wonder; the verse frames these acts as the proper object that elicits astonished praise.
The people are to come into God's presence with singing — triumphing, joyful vocalization is presented as the fitting inner disposition and outward expression when drawing near to God.
Serving the Lord is to be done with gladness — joy here is the interior quality that should animate and accompany the act of worship and service.

The term ga.vah (height/exalting) here describes the immeasurable elevation of God's steadfast love above the earth — used structurally to express that his love is loftily surpassing toward those who fear him, not inner human exultation.
Wine is said to gladden the heart of man — joy here is a felt inner state produced in the heart by a physical gift of creation, locating the gladness explicitly in the human heart.
The psalmist wishes that the Lord himself would rejoice in his own works — attributing an inner delight to God directed toward his creation as its object.
The psalmist rejoices in the Lord as a personal, voluntary act of the inner life — the joy is tied to the psalmist's meditation being pleasing to God, linking contemplation with delight.
God's wondrous works are to be told aloud in song — the verse calls for the memory and voice to rehearse acts that evoke wonder, directing the inner faculty of recollection toward them.
The hearts of those who seek the Lord are the seat of rejoicing — the inner faculty of the heart is specified as where gladness resides when one pursues God.
Israel is called to remember God's wondrous works and miracles — the inner faculty of memory is engaged toward God's past acts as the proper object of wondering recollection.
Egypt's gladness at Israel's departure is driven by dread — the inner joy here is relief-based, born of fear being lifted, showing gladness can arise from the removal of a threatening burden.
God brought his chosen people out of Egypt with joy and singing — rejoicing is presented as the accompanying inner and vocal state of the redeemed as they are led to freedom.
The Lord's goodness — expressed through enduring steadfast love — is given as the explicit reason for thanksgiving, grounding pleasant goodness in God's own reliable character.
The psalmist desires to personally rejoice in the gladness of God's chosen nation — the inner joy is aspirational, directed by will toward shared communal delight in belonging to God's people.
The psalmist longs to rejoice in the gladness of God's nation — joy here is a communal quality the psalmist desires to share, showing it as something experienced in solidarity with God's people.
Israel's ancestors failed to consider God's wondrous works in Egypt — the absence of wonder is shown as a failure of inner perception and memory, implying wonder requires conscious attention.

God's wondrous works in Ham and awesome deeds at the Red Sea are referenced — the verse identifies specific historical acts as the objective content that should produce wonder in those who recall them.

God's goodness, paired with his forever-enduring steadfast love, is given as the direct ground for giving thanks — pleasantness here belongs to God's character and motivates the inner response of gratitude.

God's wondrous works to people are the proper cause for thanksgiving — the verse links the response of gratitude to recognition of God's extraordinary deeds, engaging the inner faculty of appreciation.
As in 107:8, God's wondrous works toward people call forth thanksgiving — wonder here is the proper inner recognition of God's extraordinary care that should move the will toward gratitude.
Repeated refrain calling for thanks in response to God's wondrous works — wonder is the proper inner disposition before God's acts that generates the response of thankfulness.
Seafarers personally saw God's wondrous works in the deep — wonder is grounded in direct experiential observation of God's acts in nature, engaging the faculty of sight and producing awe.

The sailors are brought to their desired, longed-for haven — pleasure here is the relief and satisfaction of reaching what was deeply wished for, showing desire fulfilled as the content of inner delight.
The sailors were glad when the storm stilled and the waters quieted — the gladness is a felt inner relief directly tied to deliverance from danger and arrival at a safe harbor.
Again God's wondrous works and steadfast love call for thanks — wonder is the proper evaluative response to God's extraordinary acts that should orient the inner being toward gratitude.
The upright see God's just acts and become glad — the inner joy is produced by moral perception, by seeing righteousness vindicated, while the wicked are silenced.
The enemy did not delight in blessing — the absence of delight in what is good reveals a corrupt inner will that actively turns away from blessing and toward cursing.
The psalmist appeals to God's steadfast love as good — the goodness here is a quality of God's covenantal love that grounds the psalmist's plea for deliverance.
Despite enemies cursing, the servant will be glad — the inner gladness is grounded in God's blessing and stands in sharp contrast to the shame of opponents, showing joy as divinely sustained.
God's great works are studied by all who delight in them — pleasure here is an inner disposition that drives active inquiry and engagement, linking delight to the will and the mind.
God has caused his wondrous works to be remembered — wonder is preserved through memory, with the verse indicating God himself ensures his extraordinary acts remain in view.
Those who practice the fear of the Lord have good understanding — the goodness here is an inner quality of wisdom and moral discernment that results from living out reverence for God.
The blessed man greatly delights in God's commandments — the inner delight is intense and directed specifically toward God's law, showing joy and obedience as inseparable in the righteous person.

As in vc_id 9400, the blessed God-fearer greatly delights in his commandments — the inner faculty of delight is wholly directed toward God's word, characterizing the righteous person's inner orientation.
It is well — good — for the man who deals generously and justly — goodness here describes the inner and relational flourishing that flows from a character marked by generosity and fairness.
God is described as seated on high — ga.vah (height/exalting) here applies to God's lofty transcendence; it does not directly evidence human inner joy but describes divine elevation above all.
The formerly barren woman becomes a joyous mother — gladness here is the inner transformation brought by God's gift of children, turning grief into overflowing joy in the heart.
God does all that he pleases — delight here belongs entirely to God's sovereign will; his inner pleasure is the sole governing principle of his actions, unrestricted by any outside agency.
God's goodness, expressed in steadfast love that never ends, is the grounds for thanksgiving — goodness here is a stable divine character quality that continually calls forth grateful response.
Taking refuge in the Lord is better than trusting in man — goodness here is a comparative quality, showing that the inner security found in God surpasses what human trust can provide.
Refuge in the Lord is better than trust in princes — goodness here again is comparative, measuring the inner security of reliance on God as superior to confidence in human authority.
The Lord's doing is marvelous in the eyes of the beholders — wonder is the inner and perceptual response to witnessing God's unexpected, redemptive act, recognized as coming from him alone.
The day the Lord has made calls for communal rejoicing and gladness — the inner joy is directed by the will ('let us rejoice') and grounded in God's specific act of making this day.
The day the Lord has made becomes the occasion and ground for communal rejoicing; the inner gladness is directed outward toward a divinely appointed moment, shared collectively.
God's essential goodness, evidenced by his enduring steadfast love, is the quality that warrants thanksgiving; the term points to an intrinsic divine attribute that draws out grateful response.

The psalmist's inner exultation in God's testimonies is placed on equal footing with the delight one feels in immense wealth, showing the soul treats divine instruction as supremely satisfying treasure.
Delighting in God's statutes is paired with not forgetting his word, linking an affective inner pleasure to an act of sustained memory and attention directed toward divine commands.
The psalmist asks God to open his eyes so he can perceive wondrous things in the law; wonder here is a capacity of inner perception that requires divine enablement to experience.
God's testimonies function as the psalmist's personal delight and also as trusted counselors, so the inner pleasure taken in them shapes decision-making and guides the will.
Understanding God's precepts leads directly to meditating on his wondrous works; wonder here is the fruit of comprehension, an inner response that flows from grasping the way of God's commands.
The psalmist's delight in God's commandments is given as the motivating reason he asks to be led in their path; inner pleasure in the commands is the engine that drives the will toward obedience.
God's rules are called good as the ground for warding off dreaded reproach; their goodness is the inner reassurance set against the threat of shame, making them a reliable source of security.
Delight in God's commandments is intensified by love for them; the inner pleasure is rooted in relational affection, not mere intellectual appreciation.

Against the unfeeling, fat-covered heart of opponents, the psalmist's delight in God's law is set as a structural opposite, showing that inner responsiveness to the law distinguishes him from those who are spiritually numb.
The law of God's mouth is better to the psalmist than vast material wealth; its goodness is experienced as a personal, inner estimation that surpasses the value of gold and silver.
Those who fear God will rejoice when they see the psalmist, because his hope in God's word is evidence of divine faithfulness; the rejoicing of others is triggered by witnessing answered trust.
The psalmist links divine mercy and continued life to the law being his delight; inner pleasure in the law is what sustains life, making delight inseparable from survival and divine compassion.
The law as delight is presented as the only thing that kept the psalmist from perishing in affliction; inner pleasure in God's word functioned as a life-preserving resource under extreme suffering.
God's testimonies are claimed as an eternal heritage, and their joy is explicitly located in the heart; this rejoicing is a deep, personal, inner possession rather than a surface emotion.
God's testimonies are described as wonderful, and that quality of wonder is what moves the soul to keep them; the inner sense of marvel becomes the motivating force behind obedient action.
Delight in God's commandments stands in direct contrast to trouble and anguish; the inner pleasure in the commands remains a constant counterweight to external and emotional distress.
The psalmist's rejoicing over God's word is compared to the exhilarating discovery of great plunder; the inner response is vivid and visceral, like the thrill of sudden, abundant gain.
Longing for salvation and delight in the law are held together; the law as delight sustains the inner person while waiting for deliverance, linking pleasure in God's word to hope.
The invitation to go to God's house provokes immediate gladness; the inner rejoicing is triggered by the prospect of communal worship, directed toward the temple as its joyful object.
Doing good is asked of God toward those who are upright in heart; the term here points to a moral quality of beneficent action that corresponds to and rewards inner uprightness.
The community's gladness is a direct response to recognizing the great things the Lord has done for them; inner joy is grounded in gratitude for experienced divine acts.
The fruit of one's own labor bringing blessing is called 'well with you'; goodness here is an experienced inner wellbeing that flows from diligent, honest work under God's blessing.
The psalmist denies his heart is lifted up in exultation or pride; the term is used negatively—the inner faculty of the heart is deliberately held back from self-aggrandizing elevation.
The psalmist does not occupy himself with things too marvelous for him; wonder here is consciously restrained—the inner faculty is disciplined not to grasp at things beyond human comprehension.
Brotherly unity is exclaimed as both good and pleasant; the goodness here is the relational and experiential quality that makes communal harmony a source of inner and social wellbeing.
Brotherly unity is celebrated as pleasant alongside being good; the pleasantness names the experiential, felt quality of harmonious community that registers as agreeable to the inner person.
The Lord is declared good as the reason for praise, and singing to his name is pleasant; God's goodness is an intrinsic attribute that makes worship the natural and fitting inner response.
Singing praise to God's name is described as pleasant; the pleasantness characterizes worship itself as an experience that is agreeable and delightful to those who engage in it.
Whatever the Lord pleases he does across all creation; his inner delight functions as sovereign will—pleasure and power are unified so that divine preference immediately produces action.

God's goodness is the stated reason for giving thanks, tied directly to his eternal steadfast love; his goodness is not a momentary quality but a permanent, relational characteristic that grounds continual gratitude.
God alone does great wonders; the term marks his acts as uniquely extraordinary, and the refrain about steadfast love shows that wonder-producing power flows from his covenant faithfulness.
Captors demand mirth and songs from the exiles; joy here is cruelly imposed as a performance requirement, exposing the inner impossibility of genuine gladness under forced captivity.
Jerusalem is set as the psalmist's highest joy; the term names the supreme object of inner gladness, so that any forgetfulness of Jerusalem would be a betrayal of one's deepest delight.
The psalmist's praise is grounded in being fearfully and wonderfully made; wonder here is an attribute of God's creative works that the soul knows intimately and that evokes worshipful awe.

After a dramatic reversal, the psalmist's words will be heard and received as pleasant; the pleasantness is an inner quality of his speech that others will recognize only when vindicated by circumstance.

God's Spirit is called good and the psalmist asks it to lead him; the goodness here is an attribute of the divine Spirit that makes its guidance reliable and the inner experience of being led beneficial.

The psalmist will meditate on God's wondrous works alongside his glorious majesty; wonder is a quality of God's deeds that sustains contemplative engagement and draws the inner mind to prolonged reflection.
God's goodness extends to all he has made, paired with mercy; the goodness is a universal, outward-reaching characteristic of God's character that generates compassion toward all creatures.
Singing praises to God is declared both good and pleasant; the goodness here names the rightness and fittingness of the act, positioning praise as intrinsically proper and rewarding.
Praising God is called pleasant alongside good and fitting; the pleasantness captures the subjective, felt agreeableness of the act of praise, making worship an inner delight and not mere duty.

God's delight is explicitly not in physical strength or human prowess; the term marks the direction of divine inner pleasure by negation—it is not drawn to outward power but implicitly to trust and faithfulness.
The children of Zion are called to rejoice in their King; the inner gladness is directed relationally toward God as sovereign ruler, making kingship the object that evokes communal exultation.
Israel is summoned to be glad in their Maker; the gladness is directed toward God specifically as creator, grounding the inner joy in the relationship between creature and the one who made them.

Knowledge is said to be pleasant to the soul when wisdom enters the heart; pleasantness here is the inner experiential quality that knowledge produces in the soul once wisdom has taken up residence in the heart.
The wicked rejoice in doing evil and delight in its perverseness; their inner gladness is directed toward moral wrongdoing, showing that rejoicing can be deeply corrupted when its object is evil.
Being glad in doing evil describes the inner disposition of those on the wrong path; their gladness is not neutral but morally inverted, directed toward harm rather than good.
Walking in the way of the good is the outcome of heeding wisdom's instruction; goodness here is the moral character of those whose paths the wise are urged to follow and emulate.
Finding favor and good success before God and man is the promised result of loyalty and faithfulness; the goodness here is relational standing and prosperity that flows from integrity of character.
Wisdom's gain is framed as surpassing silver and gold, locating its pleasantness in the inner valuation of what is truly worthwhile over material wealth.
Nothing one desires (che.phets) can match wisdom's worth; inner desire itself is the measure, and wisdom exceeds every object of that longing.
Wisdom's ways are characterized as pleasantness itself, describing an experiential quality of inner ease and delight that marks the path of those who follow her.
The father's teaching is called good, urging the son not to abandon it; the goodness resides in the quality of the instruction as a benefit to the inner person.
The husband is urged to actively rejoice in his wife, directing inner gladness toward the covenantal relationship of marriage rather than forbidden sources.
The seductress invites sensual self-indulgence, portraying delight as carnal exultation in illicit love—an inner impulse directed toward forbidden pleasure.
All that one might desire falls short of wisdom; inner desire (che.phets) is used as the benchmark, and wisdom surpasses every object the heart craves.
Wisdom is declared better than jewels, framing its goodness as superior value perceived by those who weigh it against what the world considers most desirable.
Wisdom's fruit is better than fine gold and choice silver; its goodness is an intrinsic quality of what it yields to the one who possesses it.
Personified Wisdom was God's daily delight before creation, indicating that delight here is a relational, ongoing inner pleasure God took in Wisdom's constant presence.
Wisdom's delight extends to the children of man, showing an outward-directed inner joy that finds pleasure in humanity as the object of its affection.
Folly's invitation calls forbidden, secret bread pleasant, exposing how deceptive pleasantness can make what is morally destructive feel inwardly appealing.
A wise son causes his father to rejoice, showing that a child's character directly produces inner gladness or grief in the parent who loves them.
The righteous person's hope results in joy, contrasting with the wicked whose expectation perishes; joy here is the inner fruit of a well-founded, enduring hope.
A good man obtains divine favor, where goodness describes a moral character quality that shapes one's standing before God and the reception of blessing.
It is better (tov) to be lowly with a servant than to pretend greatness and lack bread; the good here is a pragmatic, grounded well-being over empty pride.
Those who plan peace have joy as the inner reward of their constructive intention, contrasting with the deceit that fills the hearts of evil schemers.
A good word counteracts inner anxiety that weighs the heart down, showing that well-chosen speech can lift the spirit and restore inner gladness.
A good word makes a burdened heart glad, so gladness here is the inner emotional relief experienced when anxiety is displaced by encouraging speech.
The light of the righteous rejoices, using brightness as a metaphor for an inner vitality and gladness that marks those living in righteousness, in contrast to the wicked whose lamp is extinguished.
Good sense wins favor, identifying the goodness of wisdom as a quality that generates positive relational outcomes, contrasting with the ruin brought by treachery.
A good man leaves an inheritance for future generations, where goodness is a moral character that produces lasting, multigenerational benefit.
Joy, like bitterness, resides in the heart as a private inner state that no outsider can fully access or share; it is essentially solitary and personal.
Joy can end in grief even mid-laughter, revealing that inner joy is fragile and temporally bounded, not a permanent state immune to sorrow.
A good man is filled with the fruit of his ways, indicating that moral goodness generates its own satisfying inner reward through consistent righteous living.
The evil bow before the good, describing a moral quality whose ultimate social and relational vindication is that even the wicked must yield to it.
The Lord watches both evil and good, using goodness as one pole of a divine moral oversight that encompasses the full range of human conduct.
A glad heart outwardly manifests in a cheerful face, showing that inner gladness is a constitutional heart-state that visibly shapes one's countenance and spirit.
The cheerful of heart experiences a continual feast regardless of outward circumstances, contrasting with the afflicted; inner goodness of spirit transforms the perception of every day.
A simple meal with love is better than a feast with hatred; the goodness here is relational warmth that makes even sparse circumstances feel deeply satisfying.
A wise son causes his father inner gladness, while a foolish son despises his mother; parental joy is produced or denied by the moral character of the child.
Folly is a joy to the senseless person, showing that joy can be misdirected—experienced as inner pleasure in what is actually harmful due to lack of understanding.
A timely word is declared good, identifying the apt, well-timed response as something that brings genuine inner satisfaction both to speaker and hearer.
Making an apt answer brings joy to the one who speaks it, showing that joy here is the inner satisfaction of having communicated rightly and helpfully.
Gracious (pleasant) words are declared pure before the Lord, contrasting with the wicked's abominable thoughts; pleasantness here marks speech that is morally wholesome and inwardly sincere.
Good news refreshes the bones, showing that hearing what is truly good penetrates to the body's depths, reviving the whole inner and physical person.
The light of the eyes rejoices the heart, meaning that what one perceives visually can directly stir inner gladness, affecting the heart at its emotional center.
A little gained with righteousness is better than great revenue from injustice; the goodness here is a moral quality that reframes what counts as true gain.
Getting wisdom is far better than gold; the goodness of wisdom is presented as the supreme value worth choosing above any material treasure.
It is better to have a lowly spirit with the poor than to share plunder with the proud; the goodness is the moral and inner well-being found in humility over arrogant gain.
Gracious words are like honey—pleasantness that enters and nourishes soul and body alike, showing that pleasant speech has a healing, restorative inner impact.
The violent man leads his neighbor in a way that is not good, using the negation of tov to mark a path that damages rather than benefits the inner and outward life.
A dry morsel in quiet is better than a feast with strife; the goodness is the inner peace of a simple life free from conflict, which surpasses material abundance marred by discord.
Being glad at another's calamity is a moral failing that will not go unpunished; this gladness is directed toward harm, exposing joy as morally culpable when its object is another's suffering.
Making one's door high is linked to seeking destruction; ga.vah here denotes arrogant self-exaltation rather than genuine inner joy, warning that such pride leads to ruin rather than flourishing.
The father of a fool has no joy, showing that the absence of gladness in a parent is the direct inner consequence of a child's persistent foolishness.

A joyful heart acts as good medicine, demonstrating that gladness is a constitutional inner state with tangible physical and spiritual healing effects, contrasted with the desiccating power of a crushed spirit.
Fining a righteous man is declared not good, using tov's negation to identify injustice toward the upright as a moral violation of what is truly right and beneficial.
A fool takes no delight in understanding, only in airing opinions; the absence of inner delight in wisdom reveals a self-absorbed orientation that forecloses genuine learning.
Showing partiality to the wicked is not good; tov here marks the standard of moral rightness that just treatment requires, with its absence signaling corruption of inner moral judgment.
The heart's inner elevation—exulting pride—is the direct precursor to destruction; the verse locates this haughty swelling within the heart itself as the inner condition that brings ruin.
Being 'better' here is a moral-comparative verdict: the pleasant condition is walking in integrity even in poverty, set against the inner corruption of crooked speech and foolishness.
The verse uses 'not good' to signal that desire acting without knowledge is an inner deficiency; pleasantness/goodness requires knowledge guiding desire, not impulsive haste.
Luxury (ta.a.nug) is cast as something unfitting for a fool—the delight of ease and indulgence is misaligned with the fool's character, making the pleasant life a matter of inner fitness.
The 'better' judgment here identifies steadfast love as the most desirable inner quality in a person—more pleasant and preferable than wealth or fine words.
False scales are declared 'not good'—the pleasant/good is the opposite of deceptive dealing; the verse frames moral integrity in commerce as the standard of what is truly good.
God's sovereign will directs the king's heart wherever he pleases (cha.phets); the king's deepest desires and intentions are in God's hand, redirected according to divine delight.
The 'better' condition is solitary rooftop living over domestic strife; the pleasant is inner peace and quiet, contrasted with the ongoing agitation of a quarrelsome household.
Justice being done produces joy as an inner response in the righteous—it satisfies something deep in those aligned with right, while producing terror in evildoers.
Joy (sim.chah) here appears as the object of love—loving pleasure as a life pursuit leads to poverty; the verse warns against making inner delight in pleasure one's governing desire.
Living in a desert is judged better (more pleasant) than enduring a quarrelsome woman; the verse values inner tranquility and peace over domestic comfort without relational harmony.
Favor (goodwill from others) is better than silver or gold—the pleasant condition here is the relational warmth and regard one carries, valued above material wealth.
A 'bountiful eye'—an inner disposition of generosity—is the good/pleasant quality; the verse connects this inward orientation with blessing and sharing with the poor.
Keeping wisdom's words within oneself is described as pleasant—the inner lodging of these truths in the person brings an inward pleasantness, ready to overflow on the lips.
A son's inner wisdom produces gladness in the father's heart; the rejoicing is relational and reciprocal—the child's inner condition directly gladdens the parent's inner being.
A father whose son is righteous will greatly rejoice—the child's moral character is the direct cause of the father's deep inner exultation and delight.
The father who fathers a wise son will be glad in him—gladness is the inner response to a child's wisdom, directed specifically toward the person of the son.
The mother who bore a wise son is called to rejoice—her inner joy is rooted in the fruit of her child's life, making rejoicing a natural response to the child's goodness.
Both father and mother are urged to be glad—the child's wisdom becomes the basis for parental gladness, framing joy as an inward response appropriate to a child's righteous character.
The heart is specifically named as the site where gladness over an enemy's fall must be restrained; the verse prohibits inner rejoicing directed against one who stumbles.
The command 'do not rejoice' targets the inner impulse to exult at an enemy's downfall; rejoicing here is an inner orientation of the will that must be consciously withheld.
Partiality in judging is 'not good'—the pleasant/good requires impartiality; the verse contrasts judicial integrity as the inner standard against the distortion of showing favoritism.
Those who rebuke the wicked will have delight—the inner pleasantness comes as a reward for moral courage, directed toward those willing to confront wrongdoing.
Being invited to a higher place is better than being publicly demoted; the pleasant condition here is the inward dignity of earned honor rather than presumptuous self-promotion.
Solitary living on a rooftop corner is better than a shared house with strife; the pleasant is identified with peace and inner quiet over relational conflict.
Good news from far away is 'good' like cold water to a thirsty soul—the pleasant refreshes the inner person, satisfying a deep need in one who has been longing and waiting.
Eating too much honey is 'not good'—excess, even of pleasant things, ceases to be good; the verse cautions against overindulgence and vainglorious self-seeking.
Open rebuke is better than hidden love—the truly good/pleasant is honest confrontation that serves a person, not unexpressed affection that leaves them in error.
Oil and perfume gladden the heart through the senses; the verse parallels this with a friend's earnest counsel, locating gladness as an inner response both to sensory delight and relational wisdom.
A nearby neighbor in crisis is better than a distant brother—the good/pleasant is reliable nearness and relational availability, valued above family ties weakened by distance.
A son's wisdom makes the father's heart glad; gladness here is the inner relational fruit of the child's right choices, giving the parent joy and a defense against reproach.
Integrity in poverty is better than crooked wealth—the good/pleasant is an inner moral uprightness that surpasses external prosperity gained through corrupt means.
Showing partiality is 'not good'—the pleasant/good requires equal justice; the verse names favoritism as an inner moral failure opposed to what is truly beneficial.
When the righteous thrive, the people rejoice—joy is presented as a communal inner response to just leadership, contrasted with the groaning that wicked rule produces.
Loving wisdom makes the father glad—the son's inner pursuit of wisdom is the direct source of a father's rejoicing, while its opposite (chasing prostitutes) destroys both relationship and wealth.
The righteous man sings and rejoices—his rejoicing flows from freedom from the snare of sin; the joy is an inner disposition of liberation set against the entrapment of the wicked.
Working with 'willing hands' expresses pleasure (che.phets) as an inner delight in labor itself; the excellent wife's work flows from genuine inner desire and willingness, not compulsion.
Perceiving that her work is profitable is the 'good' the verse names—the woman's inner discernment recognizes value in her labor, motivating her tireless diligence through the night.
The heart tests pleasure (sim.chah) as a life experiment, but the conclusion is that it is vanity—joy pursued as a deliberate inner project of self-testing yields emptiness.
Pleasure (sim.chah) is judged as purposeless—the Preacher questions its inner value, dismissing it as offering nothing of lasting worth to the person who pursues it.
The heart's search is for what is genuinely good for humanity—the quest is an inner, wisdom-guided experiment to discover what truly satisfies during a brief human life.
Concubines are called 'the delight of the sons of man'—sensory and relational luxury is accumulated as an object of human pleasure, part of the Preacher's comprehensive self-indulgence experiment.
The heart found pleasure (sa.me.ach) in all toil—this gladness was an inner reward experienced directly by the heart in the midst of labor, kept from no desire.
The heart was kept from no pleasure (sim.chah)—joy is presented as something the heart holds and enjoys; withholding nothing, the inner person experienced the full range of delight in toil.
Nothing is better for a person than eating, drinking, and finding enjoyment in toil—the pleasant is located in present-moment inner satisfaction in work, recognized as God's gift.
To the one who pleases God, God gives wisdom, knowledge, and joy—the pleasant is a divinely granted inner condition bestowed on those in right relationship with God.
Joy (sim.chah) is a gift God gives alongside wisdom and knowledge to the one who pleases him—it is not self-generated but divinely granted, distinguishing the favored from the sinner.
Nothing is better than to be joyful and do good—the pleasant is identified with active rejoicing combined with doing good, framing the best human life as one of inner joy and moral action.
Being joyful is one of the best things a person can do in life—the verse presents rejoicing as an inner state worth pursuing, paired with doing good as the highest human activity.
Nothing is better than a man rejoicing in his work—the pleasant inner condition is finding satisfaction in one's own labor, which the verse identifies as one's appropriate lot in life.

Rejoicing in one's work is presented as the best inner disposition available to a person, grounded in accepting that work as one's appointed lot rather than anxiously grasping for knowledge of what lies beyond.

What is 'better' here is framed negatively — not having existed and thus not having witnessed evil is judged superior to life; the pleasant/good is defined by absence of suffering, not by any positive inner experience.
What is 'better' here is inner quietness with modest means, contrasted with exhausting, double-handed toil; the pleasant quality lies in restful sufficiency rather than striving abundance.
Partnership is judged better because it yields a good reward from shared toil; the pleasant quality is the relational and practical benefit of companionship over isolated effort.
A poor but wise youth is judged better than a powerful but unteachable old king; the 'better' quality resides in the capacity to receive counsel rather than in wealth or status.
Future generations will not rejoice in the once-celebrated leader; the absence of lasting rejoicing underscores that popular acclaim is transient and ultimately empty — vanity.

God takes no pleasure in fools who vow and delay payment; the inner-being content here is divine displeasure directed toward moral irresponsibility, revealing that true pleasure requires integrity of will.
It is better not to vow than to vow and fail to pay; the 'better' here is a matter of integrity — avoiding a rash commitment is superior to making one that the will then fails to honour.
What the writer judges 'good' is finding enjoyment in one's daily toil and eating — an inner acceptance of life's lot as given by God rather than restless striving for more.
Rejoicing in one's toil is identified as a gift of God — the capacity to find inner satisfaction in work and to accept one's lot is divinely enabled, not self-generated.
Joy is located explicitly in the heart and described as God-given occupation — God keeps the person so filled with joy in his heart that he does not dwell anxiously on the brevity of his days.

A stillborn child is judged better than a man whose soul is not satisfied with good things; the 'good' here is defined by what the soul craves — inner satisfaction is the measure of whether life's pleasantness has been experienced.

What the eyes actually see is better than the wandering of appetite — present, perceivable reality is preferable to restless inner desire that can never settle; pleasantness is found in the attainable, not the endlessly craved.

The verse questions whether anyone truly knows what is good for a person during their brief, shadow-like life; the 'good/pleasant' is presented as genuinely uncertain, deepening the existential humility of Qohelet's inquiry.
Joy/mirth is located in the heart of fools, contrasted with the wise whose heart dwells in the house of mourning; the verse implies that frivolous delight is a mark of shallow inner life, not wisdom.

The king does whatever he pleases — his inner will is sovereign and unchecked; the delight/pleasure named is the exercise of absolute royal volition, which the subject must humbly accept rather than resist.

The writer commends being joyful as the best posture under the sun, describing it as something that accompanies a person through all their toil — joy is a sustaining inner companion for the whole of mortal life.

Joy is commended as the highest good available under the sun; the writer's advocacy ('I commend joy') presents it as a reasoned inner conclusion — the wisest response to life's limits is embracing joy rather than despair.

Joy is linked to a merry heart and grounded in divine approval — eating bread with joy and drinking with a glad heart is not mere hedonism but a response to God's already-given acceptance of the person's deeds.

Wine is said to gladden life — the verse treats rejoicing as an effect that wine produces in the living person, associating it with feasting and the practical reality that material resources enable it.

A person who lives many years is urged to rejoice in all of them while remembering the coming days of darkness; the rejoicing is a deliberate inner orientation — embracing life's years rather than letting future darkness cancel present joy.

The young man is urged to let his heart cheer him and to walk in the desires of his heart, yet with awareness of future judgment; rejoicing here is a heart-driven, willful embrace of youth, bounded by moral accountability.

Old age is described as the time when a person says 'I have no pleasure in them' — the inner capacity for enjoyment drains away with advancing years, making youth the season when pleasure can genuinely be felt.

The Preacher sought words of delight — his intellectual and aesthetic pursuit was to find language that carries genuine pleasure; the inner faculty engaged is the mind searching for truth expressed in a form that delights.

Exulting and rejoicing are directed personally toward the beloved king — the inner exultation is relational, arising from being drawn into his presence and expressed in shared celebration of his love.
Rejoicing is paired with exulting and directed toward the beloved — it is an outpouring of delight at intimate closeness with the king, expressed collectively by those who love him.

The beloved is called 'truly delightful' — the pleasant quality is located in the person of the beloved and perceived through the lover's appreciative gaze, evoking a warm, satisfied sense of beauty and delight.
Love is not to be stirred or awakened until it pleases — the delight is love's own readiness or timing, implying that love has an inner will or pleasure that must be respected rather than forced.
The same adjuration as Song 2:7 — love must not be roused until it chooses to delight; the inner readiness of love itself is the condition for its proper awakening, not external compulsion.

The gladness of Solomon's heart is specifically named as the inner experience of his wedding day — joy is located in the heart and tied to the moment of bridal union, making it deeply personal and celebratory.

The beloved is addressed as both beautiful and pleasant, with all her delights — pleasantness here is an aesthetic and affective quality perceived in the whole person, evoking deep relational enjoyment.

The beloved's delights are celebrated as part of her total beauty and pleasantness — the delights name the sensory and relational pleasures she embodies, received with wonder and admiration by the lover.

Love must not be awakened until it pleases — repeated again, the delight belongs to love itself; the inner readiness of love is the only proper trigger for its awakening, underscoring love's sovereign inner timing.

God declares he does not delight in the blood of sacrificial animals — his inner disposition is one of non-pleasure toward ritual offerings made without accompanying justice, exposing the emptiness of externalized worship.
It shall be 'well' (good/pleasant) with the righteous who eat the fruit of their deeds — the pleasant outcome is a moral correspondence between inner uprightness and outward blessing.

The daughters of Zion are described as haughty — their inner exaltation/pride expresses itself outwardly in ostentatious posture and wanton glances, making this a case of self-exalting pride rather than joyful exultation.

Israel is called God's 'pleasant planting' — the delight/pleasure is God's own treasured affection for his people, making their failure to produce justice all the more a betrayal of what he cherished.
The Lord of hosts is exalted/lifted high in justice — his exaltation here is not pride but the majestic elevation of his being as holiness is demonstrated through righteous judgment.

The people's rejoicing over Rezin and Remaliah's son signals misplaced inner allegiance — their delight in foreign powers rather than in God's gentle provision is the spiritual failure being condemned.
The people are glad at dividing the spoil — their rejoicing is the spontaneous inner response to victory and abundance, compared to the uninhibited joy of harvest time experienced before God.

The nation rejoices before God with the intensity of harvest joy — the rejoicing is directed toward God as the one who multiplied the nation and increased its joy, making it an act of grateful exultation.
Joy is something God increases in the nation — it is a God-given inner abundance, multiplied alongside the people, that overflows in communal celebration before him.

The coming child's name includes 'Wonderful' — this wonder is an essential quality of his being, not merely a reaction to him; his identity inherently evokes awe and exceeds ordinary human comprehension.

The Lord does not rejoice over Israel's young men because of their pervasive godlessness — divine rejoicing is withheld as a direct consequence of moral corruption, showing that God's delight is conditioned on faithfulness.

A nursing child plays over a cobra's hole without fear — the child's carefree play/delight pictures the inner fearlessness of the coming age of peace, where natural enmity no longer threatens innocent enjoyment.

Joy accompanies the drawing of water from salvation's wells — the inner experience of rejoicing is the natural disposition of those who receive God's saving provision, making salvation itself the source of joy.
The Medes do not delight in gold — their inner disposition is indifference to material reward, making them effective instruments of judgment who cannot be bribed or deflected by wealth.

The cypress trees rejoice at the fall of the oppressor — their personified rejoicing expresses relief from threat; the inner content is the glad release felt when the source of destruction is removed.
Philistia is warned not to rejoice at the breaking of the rod that struck them — premature rejoicing over an enemy's fall is a dangerous inner error when a greater threat is yet to emerge.

Joy and rejoicing are stripped away from the fruitful field and vineyards — their absence marks total desolation; rejoicing is here defined by contrast as the living inner response that harvest and abundance normally produce.
Joy is stripped from the land along with its harvest — the inner exuberance of celebration at the vintage is silenced by divine judgment, leaving no songs, no cheers, and no festive shouting.

Pleasantness here describes the desirable, cherished plants cultivated by Israel — outward beauty and delight that cannot substitute for remembering God, making such pleasant things ultimately hollow.
Joy here is a reckless, defiant inner state — feasting and merrymaking in the face of coming death, revealing a spirit that chooses pleasure over repentance in the moment of crisis.
Rejoicing here is feverish, fatalistic exuberance — eating, drinking, and celebrating in the shadow of death, a hollow festivity that replaces mourning with bravado rather than turning to God.
The merry-hearted — those whose inner being was defined by festive gladness — are reduced to sighing when the vine fails, showing that their joy was entirely tied to material abundance.
Rejoicing expressed through musical instruments and jubilant noise is completely stilled by judgment, revealing that this communal inner exuberance depended on conditions that God has now removed.
Rejoicing is banished from the earth along with wine and joy — it is a gladness so thoroughly extinguished by judgment that even the earth itself no longer holds it.

Joy has grown dark — the inner brightness of festive happiness is described as dimming like a light, showing that judgment does not merely remove joy but actively darkens it within people and the land.

Wonder describes the extraordinary quality of what God has done — his plans, formed long ago and now fulfilled faithfully, evoke exalting praise from the psalmist as something surpassing ordinary expectation.

Rejoicing is the willing, glad inner response to God's salvation after patient waiting — the people gladly embrace it, directing their exultation specifically toward God who has acted on their behalf.

To rejoice is the natural overflow of the heart after waiting and witnessing God's salvation — it is called forth by recognition of who God is and what he has done, not by external circumstance alone.

Repose is offered as a gift to the weary — a settled, restful inner state that God provides — but the people refuse to receive it, showing that the obstacle to rest is not God's withholding but human unwillingness to hear.
Wonder here qualifies God's counsel as surpassing human understanding — his wisdom and planning are so extraordinary that they exceed what the mind can fully grasp or anticipate.
Wonder is piled upon wonder in God's coming deeds — the extraordinary nature of his actions will be so overwhelming that human wisdom and discernment will be rendered useless before them.
Wonder is the character of God's announced acts — wonders so staggering they will overturn the confidence of the wise, making human intellectual achievement pale before divine action.

To rejoice is the inner response of the meek and poor when God acts on their behalf — they exult in the Holy One of Israel, finding fresh joy that comes specifically from their connection to him rather than from earthly gain.
Joy here is fresh and increasing — the meek obtain it anew in the Lord, indicating it is not merely emotional relief but a renewed inner delight sourced in God's person and his regard for the humble.
Joy here is located in the heart — gladness of heart is compared to the festive song of a night holy feast, a deep inner warmth and celebration sparked by approaching God at his mountain.
Delight here is the appeal of fertile fields and productive vineyards — their loss calls for mourning, showing that their pleasantness was something people valued and took satisfaction in.
Rejoicing describes the exultant character of city-dwellers — their houses are called joyous, reflecting an inner quality of festive gaiety that will be overtaken by desolation and thorns.

Rejoicing ironically transfers from humans to wild donkeys — once a quality of the city's inhabitants, it now belongs only to animals roaming the abandoned ruins, marking total inner desolation for the people.

To rejoice is attributed to the desert itself — the land's blossoming is described as an expression of inner gladness, showing that the coming restoration is so total that creation itself breaks into joyful response.
The dry land is called to gladness in anticipation of transformation — its rejoicing is the outward sign of a reversal so complete that even barren creation participates in the exultation.

To rejoice is the response of the blossoming land to seeing the glory of the Lord — the rejoicing is paired with abundant bloom and song, directed toward the divine majesty that causes the transformation.
Rejoicing is the quality that accompanies abundant blossoming and singing — it is the inner exuberance of the land's renewal, ultimately tied to beholding the glory and majesty of God.

Joy is everlasting and rests upon the heads of the redeemed like a crown — it is an enduring inner state accompanying their return to Zion, standing in direct contrast to sorrow and sighing that flee before it.
Gladness is something the ransomed obtain — acquired through redemption and return — and it stands as the inner counterpart to sorrow and sighing which are driven away in its coming.
Pleasant here qualifies conduct done rightly before God — Hezekiah appeals to having done what is good in God's sight, linking pleasantness to moral integrity and wholehearted faithfulness.

Rejoicing here is Hezekiah's glad inner welcome of the Babylonian envoys — a pleased, enthusiastic reception that then leads him to reveal all his treasures, showing joy motivating an imprudent openness.

Pleasant here reflects Hezekiah's self-serving inner assessment of the prophetic word — he finds it good because it means peace in his own lifetime, revealing a heart oriented toward personal comfort rather than the welfare of his descendants.

Good here describes idolaters' self-congratulating judgment about their soldering work — the pleasantness is a craftsman's satisfaction in workmanship, ironically applied to an idol that cannot move or act.
To rejoice in the Lord is the inner outcome of God's vindication of Israel against its enemies — after victory, the whole person turns in exultation toward God as the source and object of that joy.

The Lord's delight in his own righteousness drives his purpose to magnify and glorify his law — his pleasure is volitional and purposeful, not merely emotional, directing a deliberate act of glorification.
God's pleasure here is expressed as sovereign purpose — Cyrus will fulfill all that God purposes, showing that divine delight is inseparable from divine will and the accomplishment of a cosmic plan.
God's purpose — his settled will and pleasure — is declared as what stands and will be accomplished, revealing that divine pleasure is identical with sovereign intention that cannot be thwarted.

Dainty describes Babylon's former cherished, pampered status — its delicate luxury is about to be stripped away as judgment removes the tender ease that defined its self-perception and inner identity.

God's purpose here is his intentional will directed against Babylon — it is something he performs through a chosen agent, showing that divine pleasure is expressed through purposeful, history-shaping action.

To rejoice is called forth from the heavens as a response to God's compassion on his afflicted people — the inner exultation is directed outward in song and is grounded in the reality of divine comfort and mercy.

Joy will be found in the restored Zion alongside gladness and song — it is a quality that inhabits the renewed place as God's comfort transforms waste into garden, making joy an outcome of divine compassionate action.

Gladness is paired with joy in the restored Zion, both found there as gifts of divine comfort — together they characterize the inner state of a people and place transformed from desolation to Eden-like flourishing.
Joy is everlasting and rests on the heads of the ransomed returning to Zion — it is an enduring inner crown carried by those whom God has redeemed, directly displacing sorrow and sighing.

Gladness is something the ransomed obtain as they return — it accompanies joy and together they replace grief, showing that this inner state is acquired through redemption and the experience of God's salvation.

Good news of happiness here is the pleasant, joy-generating content of the message of salvation and peace — the pleasantness of the message is tied entirely to its content: God reigns and brings redemption.

To exult here describes the servant's elevation — he is lifted high and exalted, so the term captures the triumphant, elevated state that results from God's vindication of the one who acted wisely on his behalf.

The Lord's delight — his sovereign will — is what drives the servant's crushing and suffering; it is not arbitrary cruelty but purposeful divine resolve, showing that delight here is inseparable from redemptive intention.

God's pleasure here is expressed as sovereign purpose accomplished through the servant's suffering — his will to crush is the same will that causes the servant's days to prosper, linking delight to purposeful, redemptive outcome.

To delight is the inner satisfaction that comes from eating what is genuinely nourishing — people are called to direct their delight toward what truly satisfies rather than spending themselves on what cannot.

The term for exalting height here describes God's ways and thoughts as incomparably higher than human ones — it conveys transcendent elevation rather than personal exultation, with no inner-being joy content evident in this verse.

God's delight or purpose is what his word is sent to accomplish — the word will not return without fulfilling what he intends, showing that delight here is sovereign, purposeful will that drives divine speech to its goal.

Joy is the manner of Israel's going out — they will depart in joy, making it not merely an accompanying feeling but the very mode and character of their liberation, outwardly echoed by singing mountains and clapping trees.
Delighting in God is expressed through active choice — the eunuchs choose the things that please him, showing that inner delight aligns the will toward what God values.
The pleasant thing here is an everlasting name given by God within his house — an enduring honor that surpasses even the natural blessing of children.
God promises to make the outsiders joyful within his house of prayer, locating rejoicing as a gift experienced in the context of worship and divine acceptance.
The people outwardly delight to seek God and know his ways, but the verse exposes this as hollow performance — their delight lacks the moral integrity that genuine inward delight requires.
On the very day of fasting, the people pursue their own pleasure and oppress workers — inner desire is directed entirely toward self-interest, nullifying the fast's spiritual intent.
Pursuing one's own pleasure on the Sabbath is set against honoring God's holy day — the inner will must be redirected away from self-driven desire toward reverence for God.
The Sabbath is to be called a delight — an inner disposition of gladness toward God's rest-day rather than a burden, standing opposite to the self-seeking pleasure also named in the verse.
Taking delight in the Lord is the soul's response to honoring the Sabbath, and it results in God exalting the person — inner delight directed toward God produces outward blessing.
Jerusalem, once forsaken and despised, will be made a source of rejoicing forever — the inner quality of communal gladness replaces prior shame as God transforms her standing.
Gladness is given as oil poured over mourners — an inward emotional transformation replacing grief, bestowed by God to lift the spirit from faintness to praise.
Everlasting joy is promised as permanent replacement for shame and dishonor — the inner state of gladness is rooted in restored standing and an enduring divine inheritance.
The soul exults in God because he has clothed the speaker with salvation and righteousness — rejoicing is located in the soul and directed toward God as its source and object.
Rejoicing is the speaker's whole-person response to being clothed by God with salvation — the joy is intense and directed toward the Lord as its cause and ground.
God's delight in Zion is so defining that it becomes her new name — the inner disposition of divine pleasure toward her reverses her identity from forsaken to beloved.
God's rejoicing over Zion is compared to a bridegroom's gladness over his bride — the rejoicing is relational and outward-directed, expressing deep personal pleasure in the restored relationship.
God will rejoice over restored Zion as a bridegroom over a bride — the rejoicing is intense relational delight directed from God toward his people at the moment of reunion.
God meets the one who joyfully works righteousness — rejoicing is expressed through active, glad obedience, characterizing the inner disposition of those who live in alignment with God.
The people walk in a way that is not good — pleasantness here is negated, highlighting that their chosen path lacks the wholeness and rightness that 'good' implies.
God announces that the people chose what he did not delight in — their will was persistently directed away from what pleased God, and judgment follows as the consequence.
Same verse as above: the people's choices were things God did not delight in — the absence of divine delight toward their actions signals the moral distance that brought punishment.
God's servants will rejoice while the disobedient are put to shame — rejoicing is presented as a distinguishing mark of the faithful, standing in sharp contrast to the disgrace of the rebellious.
God commands rejoicing forever in the new creation, grounding the inner response of gladness in the act of divine re-creation — the command calls the will to sustained, enduring delight.
Jerusalem herself is created to be a rejoicing — the city embodies God's intent for communal exultation as the purpose and fruit of the new creation.
God creates the people of Jerusalem to be a gladness — their corporate inner joy is not self-generated but divinely designed as an expression of what the new creation produces.
The command to rejoice is grounded in God's act of creating something new — the call to rejoice is directed inward and sustained, motivated by what God is doing rather than circumstances.
God himself will rejoice in Jerusalem — the divine inner response of gladness is directed toward his city and people, and it displaces weeping and distress entirely.
God will be glad in his people — the rejoicing is God's own inner delight directed toward the restored community, standing opposite to the crying and distress that will be silenced.
The soul delights in abominations — the inner faculty of desire is directed toward what God abhors, showing that delight can be misdirected toward corrupt objects.
God did not delight in what the people chose — his inner disposition was set against their evil choices, and judgment follows their persistent rejection of his call.
Those who hate the faithful mockingly predict they will see their joy, but shame will fall on the mockers instead — the faithful's joy, vindicated by God, is a witness against their opponents.
All who love Jerusalem are called to rejoice with her — the inner gladness is communal and sympathetic, directed toward the city by those who share in her restoration.
Those who mourn over Jerusalem are called to rejoice with her in joy — the inner state of gladness is the appointed response to her restoration, replacing grief for those who loved her.
Those who love Jerusalem are called to rejoice with her — the rejoicing is relational, shared among those who have mourned, now invited into communal delight at her vindication.
The call to rejoice with Jerusalem is addressed to all who love her — gladness is the appropriate inner response to her restoration, especially for those who mourned her desolation.
Delighting is the experience of deeply drinking from Jerusalem's abundant consolation — the inner satisfaction is portrayed as rich, physical pleasure drawn from God's gift of restoration.

Being bounced on the knees depicts the delight of an infant cradled in comfort — the inner experience is carefree, tender pleasure received from a nurturing, peaceful relationship with God's renewed city.
The heart shall rejoice at seeing God's vindication of his servants — gladness is located explicitly in the heart as the inner response to witnessing God's saving power.
The people take no pleasure in God's word, their ears uncircumcised and closed — the absence of delight in his word signals a deep inner alienation from God that makes repentance impossible.
The good way is identified as the ancient path where rest for the soul is found — pleasantness here is the wholesome quality of the right path that brings inner rest.
God will silence the voice of gladness in Judah's cities — joy as a communal, expressed emotion is removed as judgment, its absence marking desolation and the end of normal life.
The voice of mirth is silenced along with gladness — rejoicing as communal celebration is extinguished by divine judgment, leaving only waste where festivity once rang out.
The prophet's cheer — his inner capacity for comfort — is utterly gone, replaced by grief and a sick heart; the absence of this quality intensifies the weight of the people's devastation.
God delights in steadfast love, justice, and righteousness — his inner disposition is oriented toward these moral realities, and knowing this is the only legitimate ground for human boasting.
Pride is warned against as God has spoken — here exulting in oneself is the opposite of humble attentiveness; the term appears negated, showing that inner haughtiness blocks hearing God.
God's words became joy and the delight of Jeremiah's heart when he received them — inner gladness is located specifically in the heart and springs from the personal experience of God's word.
God's word became a rejoicing alongside the delight of Jeremiah's heart — the rejoicing is an inward, personal exultation that accompanies the soul-satisfying reception of divine speech.
God will silence the voice of gladness before the people's eyes — the removal of this audible, communal joy is a sign of coming judgment, its loss marking the end of normal social life.

The voice of mirth, silenced by God's judgment, represents the communal rejoicing of ordinary life — its removal signals that celebration and inner festivity will cease entirely in the coming devastation.
The father was made very glad by news of a son's birth — the rejoicing is an intense, natural emotional response to good news, now cursed because of the suffering Jeremiah's life has brought.

The petitioners hope God will act according to his wonderful deeds — wonder here points to extraordinary divine acts that inspire awe; the verse looks for God to reproduce such marvels in their crisis.

Wellbeing ('tov') here is the inner and outward flourishing that followed Josiah's practice of justice and righteousness — not from wealth or cedar palaces but from doing right, producing a life that was genuinely well.
Wellbeing ('tov') results from defending the poor and needy; the king's inner orientation toward justice produced a state of things that was genuinely good, and God equates this with knowing him.
The term 'pleasure/desire' appears negatively: Coniah is likened to a vessel for which no one cares, meaning no one has any delight or desire for him, marking his utter inner and social rejection.

Joy ('sim.chah') here is expressed as communal vocal celebration — the gladness of weddings and daily life — which God threatens to silence entirely as judgment, showing it as a living inner-social reality now subject to removal.

Rejoicing ('sa.s.von') is paired with mirth and embodied in joyful sound; God's judgment banishes this voice, depicting rejoicing as an audible inner-communal expression that can be stripped from a people.

The 'good' promise ('tov') here is God's declared intention to restore the exiles after seventy years — a future wellbeing that the inner person is to hope in, grounding present endurance in a coming good.

Joy ('sim.chah') here is the inner ground for commanded singing and shouting — the people are called to express gladness for Jacob's coming restoration, making joy the motivating emotion behind corporate praise.
Rejoicing ('sa.mach') describes the young women's dancing delight in the coming restoration, a visceral inner gladness expressed bodily — God himself initiates it by turning mourning to joy.

Rejoicing ('sa.s.von') is given by God as a direct replacement for sorrow — it is the inner emotional state he pours into his people as comfort, paired explicitly against mourning and sorrow as its opposite.

Delight ('sha.a.shu.im') is used of God's tender attachment to Ephraim as a darling child — an inner cherishing that persists even through rebuke, stirring God's heart to yearn and his will to show mercy.

The term 'too hard/wonderful' ('pa.la') is used to assert that nothing surpasses God's power — Jeremiah's prayer recognizes that no task lies beyond God, framing wonder as the acknowledgment of limitless divine capacity.

God echoes Jeremiah's confession: nothing is too wonderful/hard for him — the rhetorical question claims that his own deeds never exceed his power, positioning the wonder-concept as a marker of his absolute sovereignty.

Rejoicing ('su.s') describes God's own inner delight in doing good to his people — rooted in all his heart and soul, it is the deepest volitional and affective commitment he has toward their restoration.

Rejoicing ('sa.s.von') is the inner-renown quality Jerusalem will carry — she becomes a name that evokes joy in God before all nations, as his good deeds toward her generate awe and gladness in observers.

Goodness ('tov') here is a divine attribute declared in worship — the Lord is good, meaning his character grounds the gratitude of the restored people, and his goodness is the reason thanksgiving returns.

Joy ('sim.chah') is paired with mirth as the voice of restored communal celebration, heard again in Jerusalem after desolation — its return signals that God has reversed judgment and given back the inner life of the community.

Rejoicing ('sa.s.von') is depicted as a joyful sound returned to the city after silence — its reappearance alongside the bridegroom's voice marks the restoration of communal inner gladness that judgment had taken away.

The 'good promise' ('tov') is God's pledged word of restoration to Israel and Judah — the inner quality here is trustworthiness and beneficence: God's declared intention is inherently good and will be fulfilled.

Good ('tov') is used in practical discernment — what seems good and right to Jeremiah's own judgment determines his choice of destination; inner personal assessment of what is beneficial guides the will to act.
Rejoicing ('sa.mach') is the spontaneous inner relief felt by the captives when they saw Johanan's forces arrive — a sudden emotional gladness triggered by rescue from Ishmael's threat.

To desire/delight in ('cha.phets') here describes the people's inner drive to go to Egypt — their strong personal wish to live there is the very place where disaster will find them, so desire leads to destruction.

Wellbeing ('tov') is what the people believed they had when worshipping the queen of heaven — a remembered prosperity equated in their minds with the pleasantness of life before Josiah's reform, driving their resolve to return to idolatry.

Gladness ('gil') has been stripped from Moab's fertile land — once expressed as harvest shouts at the winepress, it is now silenced by divine judgment, marking joy as a communal inner reality attached to abundance and now removed.

Joy ('sim.chah') is named as the first thing taken away from Moab's fruitful land — its removal alongside gladness signals total inner desolation, contrasted against the shouting that once filled the harvest.

Pleasure/desire ('che.phets') is absent: Moab is a broken vessel for which no one has any care or desire — the term marks complete inner rejection, where even God expresses no pleasure in what Moab has become.

Exulting ('ga.vah') in self-elevation — nesting as high as the eagle — is exposed as a self-deception; the pride of the heart that soars in arrogance will be brought down by God, showing exultation in one's own height as a fatal inner error.

Rejoicing ('ma.s.vo.s') characterizes Damascus as a city of joy — the speaker's deep inner attachment to the city makes its threatened abandonment a cause of grief, joy here being the quality of life and delight the city once embodied.
Rejoicing ('sa.mach') here describes Babylon's inner exultation over plundering God's people — a glad triumphalism that God will ultimately judge, so the rejoicing carries an implicit condemnation.

Delicacies/delight ('ed.nah') refers to Jerusalem's choicest pleasures devoured by Nebuchadnezzar — the inner goods and delights of the people have been consumed by the enemy, leaving them emptied out.
Wonder/terrible ('pe.le') describes Jerusalem's fall as shocking and extraordinary in its severity — the inner impact is one of appalling astonishment at how far and how terribly she has fallen.
Rejoicing ('su.s') here is the enemy's glad response to Zion's suffering — their inner pleasure at her calamity is presented as a wrong that will be reversed when God brings the same day upon them.

Rejoicing ('ma.s.vo.s') was Jerusalem's defining communal quality — once called the joy of all the earth, her inner vitality and delight are now shockingly absent, making passersby mock what she used to be.

Rejoicing ('sa.mach') is what God has caused the enemy to experience over Zion's ruin — the enemy's inner exultation is the painful instrument of divine judgment, turning the oppressor's joy into Zion's deepest wound.
