Goodness ('tov') is attributed to the Lord as the direct experience of the waiting, seeking soul — those whose inner orientation is patient expectation of God discover his goodness personally directed toward them.

Good ('tov') here is the inner disposition of quiet waiting for God's salvation — it is genuinely beneficial for the soul to adopt silent, hopeful patience, making goodness an inner posture rather than an outward circumstance.

Good ('tov') here is the formative benefit to a young person of bearing hardship — enduring the yoke in youth is an inner discipline that produces genuine wellbeing, making difficulty a source of long-term flourishing.

Good and bad ('tov') are both said to come from the mouth of the Most High — the verse frames both pleasant and painful experience as equally under God's sovereign word, removing the inner illusion that only good comes from him.

Better off ('tov') is applied grimly: those slain by the sword are declared more fortunate than those dying of famine — the inner comparison inverts normal wellbeing, showing how extreme suffering redefines what counts as good.

Rejoicing ('su.s') is addressed to Edom with bitter irony — her present exultation over Judah's fall is real but temporary, and the command to rejoice only highlights that her own cup of judgment is coming.

Gladness ('sa.mach') is commanded to Edom in cutting irony — her inner glee over Zion's ruin is acknowledged, but the verse immediately undercuts it with the warning that she too will drink the cup of wrath.

Rejoicing ('ma.s.vo.s') has ceased from the heart — the community's inner joy is explicitly located in the heart and its outward expression in dancing, and both have been extinguished, replaced by mourning.

Soothing ('ni.cho.ach') aroma was offered by Israel to idols in every high place — what was meant to be a pleasing offering to God was misdirected to false gods, making the inner act of worship a source of offense rather than delight.

Rejoicing ('sa.mach') is forbidden to the buyer in the coming day of wrath — normal inner pleasure at a good purchase is rendered meaningless because divine judgment will overturn all such distinctions.

Soothing ('ni.cho.ach') aroma was what Israel offered to idols using the food God had given her — the pleasure-offering meant for God was diverted to idols, turning what should have delighted him into an act of betrayal.

Haughtiness ('ga.vah') is linked directly to abomination before God — Sodom's inner pride expressed itself in gross sin, and God's response was immediate removal, showing that self-exaltation provokes divine rejection.

The high tree ('ga.vah') is brought low by God — what exalts itself is humbled while the low is raised, showing that God actively reverses inner and outward self-elevation, making his sovereign will the antithesis of human pride.

Not good ('tov') describes the father's harmful conduct — extortion and robbery are named as the opposite of good, meaning his inner moral orientation was away from what is genuinely beneficial, warranting death.

God's delight ('cha.phets') is not in the death of the wicked but in their repentance and life — his inner desire is directed toward restoration rather than destruction, revealing that mercy rather than judgment is his deepest pleasure.

God has no pleasure ('cha.phets') in anyone's death — his inner will is entirely oriented toward life, and the command to turn and live flows directly from this divine disposition against delight in human destruction.

Not good ('tov') describes the statutes God gave in judgment — as a consequence of rebellion, he allowed laws that could not produce life, inverting the inner benefit that his truly good commands were meant to provide.

The soothing aroma here represents Israel's illicit worship offerings made on high hills and under leafy trees—a pleasing fragrance directed not to God but to unauthorized shrines, framing the inner act of devotion as misdirected appeasement.

God frames his future acceptance of restored Israel as receiving a pleasing aroma—the inner sense here is divine delight and approval, manifesting as acceptance when the scattered people are regathered and his holiness is displayed.

The rhetorical question 'shall we rejoice?' stands against the image of a sword sharpened for slaughter—any inner exultation is exposed as impossible or obscene given the coming devastation, implying judgment forecloses joy.

The command to 'exalt what is low and bring low what is exalted' frames self-exaltation as something God actively reverses—the inner pride of elevated status is overturned by divine action, not sustained by it.

The young Assyrian men are described as 'desirable,' indicating that the inner attraction felt toward them is an aesthetic-erotic delight in their appearance and military bearing, driving Israel's spiritual adultery.
Israel's lust for the Assyrians is centered on their being 'desirable young men'—the delight is rooted in inner craving and attraction to outward military splendor, fueling her unfaithfulness.

The Babylonian and Assyrian men are called 'desirable,' indicating that the inner pull toward them is an enthralled attraction to their power and appearance, which drives Oholibah's spiritual and political betrayal.

The 'joy and glory' and 'delight of their eyes' refer to what Israel most treasures inwardly—the temple and their children—making God's removal of these things a devastating blow to the soul's deepest desires.

Ammon's rejoicing is described as arising from 'malice within your soul'—locating the rejoicing at the deepest inner level, where hatred of Israel becomes the source of a corrupt, vindictive gladness.

The prince of Tyre's heart is called 'proud,' and he has inwardly claimed divine status—this self-exaltation is located explicitly in the heart, the seat of identity, making his arrogance a fundamental distortion of inner being.

Wealth gained through wisdom caused the heart to become proud—inner exaltation here is a consequence of material success, showing how prosperity corrupts the heart's orientation toward its proper place.

The heart's pride was triggered by beauty, corrupting wisdom for the sake of splendor—inner exaltation is shown to have distorted the faculty of wisdom itself, leading to public humiliation as divine judgment.
The great tree's 'proud heart' because of its height uses the heart as the seat of arrogant self-assessment—inner exaltation over one's own grandeur is the sin that invites God's judgment of felling.

God declares he takes no pleasure in the wicked person's death but delights in their repentance and life—his inner delight is directionally oriented toward restoration, not punishment, revealing a will inclined toward mercy.

The 'good pasture' and 'clear water' are described as pleasant resources to be enjoyed, but here the concern is that leaders consume them selfishly and destroy what remains—the pleasantness of provision is contrasted with destructive greed.

While the whole earth rejoices, Edom alone will be made desolate—the universal joy of others stands as a structural contrast to Edom's isolation in judgment, making their exclusion from rejoicing a mark of divine condemnation.

Edom's joy over Israel's desolation is used by God as the very measure of their own coming desolation—the inner gladness taken in another's ruin becomes the basis and proportion of divine retribution.

The nations seized God's land with 'wholehearted joy'—their inner delight was total and undivided, combined with contempt, showing that their joy was morally corrupt, rooted in greed and disdain rather than legitimate gladness.

The youths chosen are described as of 'good appearance'—the pleasantness here is physical and intellectual attractiveness evaluated externally by the king's court, not an inner moral quality in this context.

The king was 'exceedingly glad' when Daniel was found unharmed—his inner joy is relief and delight rooted in Daniel's safety, with the verse linking that gladness directly to Daniel's trust in God as its cause.

The 'fearful destruction' wrought by the king is described with a term connoting the extraordinary or wondrous—here it signals awe-inspiring devastation beyond normal expectation, inverting wonder into dread rather than delight.

The king speaks 'astonishing things' against God—the wonder-term here describes blasphemous speech that shocks by its audacity, framing the inner presumption as producing awe-inspiring offense rather than marvel at the divine.

The question 'how long till the end of these wonders?' treats the apocalyptic events as astonishing, extraordinary occurrences that exceed normal comprehension—wonder here names the bewildering, awe-inducing character of end-time events.

Israel reflects that life with her first husband was 'better' than her current condition—the pleasantness of the former relationship is recognized in hindsight through deprivation, showing that what was once taken for granted becomes the object of longing.

God announces he will end all Israel's 'mirth'—the rejoicing associated with feasts and sacred calendars will be cut off, showing that the inner gladness of festival celebration is entirely contingent on God's continued blessing.

God declares his delight is in steadfast love and knowledge of God rather than ritual sacrifice—his inner desire is oriented toward relational faithfulness, not external performance, revealing what truly satisfies his will.

The king and princes are made glad by the people's evil and treachery—inner gladness here is morally perverted, found in wickedness rather than virtue, exposing a corrupt joy that thrives on deception.

Israel is commanded not to rejoice like other peoples because their celebration is built on spiritual prostitution—the inner rejoicing is illegitimate, its joy hollow because it rests on unfaithfulness to God.

Israel is told not to rejoice, because their festive gladness is corrupted by their harlotry in forsaking God—the inner rejoicing is forbidden on grounds that its spiritual foundation has been betrayed.

The idolatrous priests rejoiced over the golden calf and its glory, but now mourn its departure—their inner joy was bound to an idol, and its removal exposes that rejoicing founded on false worship cannot endure.

Gladness 'dries up' from the people just as the crops fail—the inner rejoicing of human life is shown to be organically connected to creation's fruitfulness, and its withering signals total communal desolation.

Joy and gladness have been cut off from God's house along with food—the verse locates rejoicing within the worship community, showing that when provision fails, the inner exultation of corporate worship disappears with it.

Joy is described as cut off from God's house together with gladness—the inner experience of joy is shown to belong to the sacred community's life before God, and its loss is as tangible and devastating as the loss of food.

The land is called to be glad and rejoice because the Lord has done great things—inner rejoicing is grounded in God's mighty acts, framing it as a response to divine action rather than an autonomous emotion.

The command to rejoice is paired with 'fear not' and grounded in God's great deeds—inner gladness is presented as the appropriate and rational response to divine intervention, displacing fear with joy.

The children of Zion are called to rejoice in the Lord their God who has restored rain—inner rejoicing is directed toward God as its object and grounded in his gift of vindication through abundant provision.

Israel is told to be glad and rejoice in the Lord because he has given rain for vindication—inner gladness here is theocentric, its proper object being God himself and his restorative acts toward his people.

God has 'dealt wondrously' with his people—the wonder-term describes God's extraordinary, beyond-ordinary acts of restoration, producing praise and the inner recognition that what he has done surpasses normal expectation.

The 'pleasant vineyards' are objects of investment and delight that the oppressors will never enjoy—the pleasantness of what they cultivated is contrasted with their coming dispossession, making delight a thing forfeited by injustice.

The rhetorical question 'are you better than these kingdoms?' uses the comparative sense of pleasantness—the verse challenges Israel's inner sense of superiority by pointing to fallen nations as a warning against complacent self-assessment.

Israel rejoices in military victories achieved by their own strength—their inner gladness is self-congratulatory and self-reliant, a boastful pride in human achievement rather than gratitude toward God.

Edom's soaring to eagle heights and nesting among stars implies inner self-exaltation—their arrogant elevation of themselves is met with God's declaration that he will bring them down, showing exaltation as the precondition of their fall.

Edom is forbidden to rejoice over Judah's ruin—the inner gladness taken in a brother's misfortune is condemned as a moral violation, revealing that such rejoicing flows from malice and betrayal rather than legitimate joy.

The sailors appeal to God by saying he has 'done as it pleased you' regarding Jonah—they acknowledge that God's inner will and sovereign delight determine outcomes, framing divine pleasure as the ultimate cause of events.

Jonah judges it 'better' to die than to live—here the comparative of pleasantness measures inner well-being, and Jonah's despair leads him to assess death as preferable, showing that his sense of what is good has been overwhelmed by anguish.

Jonah was 'exceedingly glad' because of the plant that shaded him—his inner joy is intense but physically triggered and self-directed, focused on personal comfort, contrasting sharply with God's broader compassion in the surrounding narrative.

Jonah's exceedingly great joy over the plant is an inner delight in physical relief from discomfort—the joy is immediate and sensory, rooted in a shade plant rather than in God's purpose, exposing the shallowness of self-focused gladness.

Jonah again says it is 'better' to die than to live—the comparative sense of good measures inner quality of existence, and Jonah's despairing judgment shows that his soul finds life itself unbearable, reversing any normal assessment of what is pleasant.

The children are called 'children of your delight'—the term names the deep inner attachment and tender pleasure parents take in their children, making the call to mourning shaving all the more devastating as this delight is taken into exile.

Women are driven from their 'delightful houses'—the homes are described as sources of deep comfort and pleasure, and their violent loss is an attack on the inner sense of security and delight that belongs to domestic life.
Used ironically here: the 'best' among the people offers nothing pleasant but is like a painful brier, so the inner quality of goodness/pleasantness is entirely absent from their character.
The speaker forbids the enemy from rejoicing over her fall, implying that such rejoicing would be a malicious inner triumph; her own spirit is sustained by trust that God will restore her.

God will again perform acts so extraordinary that they evoke wonder, just as the Exodus miracles did — the inner response of astonishment is stirred by witnessing divine power beyond ordinary expectation.

God's delight is directed toward steadfast love (hesed), and this inward disposition is what motivates him to pardon iniquity rather than retain anger — his delight shapes his relational will toward mercy.
The Lord's goodness is not merely a quality but a refuge-providing reality: in the day of trouble his inner character of goodness translates into protective care for those who take shelter in him.
The Babylonian conqueror's rejoicing is an inward exultation over his military haul, felt as predatory gladness at successfully netting the nations — a morally distorted delight in domination.
Paired with rejoicing (gil), the enemy's gladness is a fierce inner satisfaction at having swept up conquered peoples like fish — delight rooted in violence and power rather than virtue.
The warriors' exultation is their inner eagerness to devour the poor in secret — it is a predatory, sinister elation that drives their attack, making exultation here the emotional fuel of cruelty.

Despite total material loss (implied by context), the prophet resolves to rejoice in the Lord — the inner act of rejoicing is a willful, faith-driven choice directed entirely toward God as its object.
The 'proudly exultant ones' are those whose inner self is puffed up in arrogant self-exaltation; God promises to remove them, showing this distorted exulting is incompatible with his holy presence.
Rejoicing is commanded with 'all your heart,' locating it explicitly in the innermost faculty of the person — it is a whole-person, wholehearted inner response to God's saving acts.
God himself will rejoice over his people with loud singing — divine rejoicing is an inner delight directed toward the restored community, expressed outwardly in exultant song.

The Lord rejoices over his people with gladness — this rejoicing is God's own inner delight in his people, paired with quieting love, portraying a tender, joyful divine affection directed outward toward them.

God's joy is expressed as the gladness with which he rejoices over his people — it is a divine inner emotional state of gladness directed toward the restored community, accompanying his exultant singing.
The Lord's words are described as gracious and comforting — here 'good/pleasant' characterizes the quality of divine speech that brings inner reassurance and comfort to the recipient.
The daughter of Zion is called to rejoice because God is coming to dwell in her midst — the rejoicing is a proper inner response to the promise of divine presence, triggered by that coming reality.

Those who despised small beginnings will rejoice when they see God's purposes completed through Zerubbabel — their inner gladness reverses prior contempt and is evoked by witnessing God's faithful work.

The restoration God promises may seem marvelous (wonder-inducing) in the eyes of the remnant, but God challenges whether it should be marvelous to him — wonder here marks the limit of human inner perception versus divine capacity.

Former fasts are transformed into cheerful feasts — here 'cheerful/good' describes the quality of communal celebration that replaces mourning, naming a changed inner mood of gladness for the house of Judah.
The fasts of Judah will become seasons of joy and gladness — joy here is a communal inner state replacing grief, a gift from God transforming the calendar from mourning into celebration.
The fast days will become seasons of rejoicing, listed alongside joy and gladness — rejoicing is an inner-communal exuberance that God promises to restore in place of former sorrow and fasting.

The daughter of Zion is commanded to rejoice greatly at the coming of her humble, saving king — the rejoicing is an exuberant inner-and-expressed response called forth by the arrival of the messianic king.
The hearts of Ephraim's children will rejoice in the Lord — the inner seat (heart) is explicitly named as the location of this rejoicing, which is directed toward the Lord as its object.
Ephraim's hearts will be glad as with wine — the gladness is a heart-level inner exhilaration likened to wine's lifting effect, rooted in God's restoration of his people.

The shepherd names his staff 'Favor/Pleasantness' (No'am), symbolizing the gracious, pleasant quality of the covenant relationship he tends — pleasantness here is personified as the character of divine favor toward the flock.

Breaking the staff named Favor/Pleasantness symbolizes the annulling of the covenant — the pleasantness that characterized divine favor is cut off, marking the end of that gracious relational quality.

The shepherd appeals to the people's judgment — 'if it seems good to you' — inviting an inner assessment of what is right or fitting, but the response of thirty pieces of silver signals contemptuous indifference.
God declares he has no pleasure in the priests or their offerings — his inner disposition is one of complete rejection, refusing to take delight in worship rendered without sincerity or devotion.

The people cynically claim God delights in evildoers — this is a distorted accusation projecting onto God an inner approval of evil, which the verse frames as spiritual weariness brought upon the Lord.
The accusation that God 'delights in them' (evildoers) is the offensive claim being refuted — delight here is the inner approval wrongly attributed to God, used to challenge his justice.
The people cynically call evildoers 'good in the sight of the Lord' — here 'good/pleasant' is used ironically as a false moral judgment, inverting the proper inner recognition of goodness.

The people delight in the coming messenger of the covenant — this inner delight is directed toward the anticipated divine envoy, representing a longing or desire that is ironically about to confront them.
Israel will be called a 'land of delight' — pleasure/delight here describes the quality that the restored land will embody in the eyes of all nations, reflecting God's favor resting on it.
Seeing the star, the magi rejoiced exceedingly — the rejoicing is an intense, overflowing inner response triggered by the visible sign that confirms the fulfillment of their seeking.
The magi's rejoicing was accompanied by 'great joy' — the joy is an intensified inner state of gladness, magnified by the word 'great,' evoked by seeing the confirming star.
The Father declares he is well pleased with the Son at his baptism — divine delight is directed toward Jesus personally, expressing an inner approval rooted in the Father-Son relationship.

The persecuted are commanded to exult (agalliao) in the face of suffering, because their reward is great in heaven — this exulting is a willful, faith-driven inner triumph that transcends present pain.
Jesus commands those who are persecuted to rejoice — the rejoicing is a counter-intuitive, commanded inner response rooted not in circumstances but in the certainty of heavenly reward.

Jesus tells the paralytic to 'take heart' upon pronouncing forgiveness — take heart here is an inner shift from anxiety or despair to courage and assurance, grounded in the experience of sins being forgiven.
Jesus tells the woman 'take heart' as he affirms her healing — the inner state of courage and confidence is restored to her through his word, replacing the fear or shame she carried.
The Father says 'my soul is well pleased' with the chosen servant — delight is located explicitly in the soul (psychē), the deepest inner being of God, directed wholly toward the Son.
The rocky-ground hearer receives the word immediately with joy — this joy is an initially genuine but shallow inner response that, lacking root, cannot sustain the person through hardship.
In his joy the man sells everything to buy the field with the treasure — joy here is the inner motivating force that drives radical, whole-life action toward the kingdom of heaven.
Jesus commands the fearful disciples on the water to 'take heart' at his self-revelation — the inner shift from fear to courage is grounded entirely in recognizing his presence and identity.
At the Transfiguration the Father again declares he is well pleased with the Son and adds 'listen to him' — divine delight in Jesus is the basis on which his authority to be heard is grounded.

The shepherd rejoices more over the one recovered sheep than the ninety-nine safe ones — this rejoicing is an intense inner gladness at restoration, revealing the relational value placed on the one who was lost.

The faithful servant is invited to 'enter into the joy of your master' — joy here is a shared relational state of the master's own inner gladness, into which the servant is welcomed as a reward for faithfulness.
Repeated to the second faithful servant, 'enter into the joy of your master' — joy is again the master's own inner gladness presented as the ultimate destination and reward of faithful stewardship.

The women left the tomb with 'fear and great joy' simultaneously — joy is a powerful inner state arising from the resurrection news, co-existing with fear, and propelling them urgently to tell the disciples.

The Father's voice declares he is well pleased with Jesus at the baptism — this divine delight is an inner approval directed toward the Son personally, affirming his identity and mission at the outset.
The crowd's inner state is seized with astonishment at Jesus' authoritative command over unclean spirits, producing a bewildered questioning among themselves about the nature of what they witnessed.
Joy is the immediate inner response when the word is heard, but here it is surface-level and rootless, not anchored deep enough to sustain faith under pressure.
Jesus commands an inner shift from terror to courage by identifying himself, directing the disciples to replace fear with heartened confidence grounded in his presence.
The disciples' inner being is struck with amazement at Jesus' words about wealth and the kingdom, revealing that his teaching exceeded their existing understanding and expectations.
Those walking with Jesus toward Jerusalem are inwardly astonished by his resolute advance, a reaction set against the fear of those following behind, showing a complex mixture of awe and dread.
The command to take heart is directed to a blind beggar in distress, calling his inner being to shift from anxious crying out to confident expectation upon learning Jesus is summoning him.
The chief priests' gladness is a morally dark inner delight triggered by the opportunity to betray Jesus, showing that this rejoicing serves treacherous intent rather than anything good.
Joy is promised as a personal inner possession for Zechariah alongside gladness, and it will radiate outward so that many others also rejoice at John's birth.
Many people will rejoice at John's birth, showing that his coming produces a shared outward expression of inner gladness spread widely across those who hear of it.
Joy is a personal inner gift promised to Zechariah at his son's birth, paired with gladness and anticipated as a reality that will also extend to many others.
The angel's greeting 'Rejoice' addresses Mary's inner being as one highly favored and accompanied by the Lord, making gladness the fitting posture for one so blessed.
Joy is so physically immediate and intense that it caused the unborn baby to leap in the womb at the sound of Mary's greeting, locating this exultation at the deepest bodily-spiritual level.
Mary's rejoicing is explicitly located in her spirit and directed toward God as Savior, making this a deep inner, worshipful exultation arising from the soul's recognition of divine salvation.
Neighbors and relatives share in the joy at Elizabeth's mercy from the Lord, showing that rejoicing here is a communal inner response that bonds people together around God's gracious act.
Joy is described as great news itself — the announcement of Christ's birth is characterized as great joy meant for all people, making joy the defining inner content of the gospel proclamation.
The Father's delight is expressed as a declared pleasure in the beloved Son at his baptism, showing an approving inner disposition from the Father directed personally toward Jesus.
Rejoicing is commanded as the inner response to persecution, grounded not in present circumstances but in the assured great reward in heaven, redirecting the will toward eschatological hope.
Joy is the immediate inner welcome given to the word, but without root it cannot sustain; the verse exposes joy that is real but shallow, unable to endure testing and falling away.
Pleasures of life are presented as competing inner appetites that choke the word, diverting the person's desires away from fruitfulness toward worldly gratification.
The seventy-two return with an exhilarated, mission-born joy rooted in their experience of spiritual power over demons exercised in Jesus' name.
Jesus redirects rejoicing from the thrill of power over spirits toward the deeper, lasting cause of having one's name written in heaven, reordering the object of inner delight.
Jesus' rejoicing is located specifically in the Holy Spirit and expressed as grateful praise to the Father, making this an exultation of the spirit empowered and directed by the Spirit of God.
The Father's good pleasure to give the kingdom is the ground for the disciples' fearlessness, showing that divine delight is a generous, active willing toward the flock's ultimate good.
The people's rejoicing at Jesus' glorious deeds stands in contrast to his adversaries' shame, showing joy here as a communal inner response to witnessing divine power and liberation.
The shepherd rejoices as he carries the found sheep home on his shoulders, making joy the natural inner overflow of a successful rescue that immediately accompanies the recovery.
The shepherd calls friends and neighbors to share his joy over finding the lost sheep, showing that this inner delight is so full it cannot be contained but must be shared communally.
Heaven's joy over one repentant sinner exceeds that over the ninety-nine who need no repentance, locating this joy in God's inner response to restored relationship through repentance.
The woman invites her friends and neighbors to share her inner joy at finding the lost coin, again showing that personal delight at recovery instinctively seeks communal expression.
Joy exists before the angels of God over one sinner's repentance, placing this joy in a heavenly, divine sphere that responds to the inner turning of a human soul back to God.
Gladness is declared fitting and necessary because restoration of the lost — death to life, lost to found — makes celebration and joy the only appropriate inner and communal response.
Zacchaeus receives Jesus joyfully, his inner delight expressed through eagerness and haste, showing that joy here is the heart's welcome of the one who chose to stay at his house.

The disciples' rejoicing bursts forth loudly in praise of God, driven by all the mighty works they personally witnessed, making their joy an outward eruption of inner conviction about what they had seen.
